statement: [arXiv:1804.01529 Lemma 2.2 and equation (11)] Let ℓ,r>0 and define the quartic polynomial Q_{ℓ,r}(x)=∑_{i=0}^4 q_i x^i by q0=1, q1=2r²(2ℓ+r)/(ℓ(ℓ+r)³), q2=r(−8ℓ²−ℓr+r²)/(ℓ²(ℓ+r)³), q3=(4ℓ²−4ℓr−2r²)/(ℓ²(ℓ+r)³), and q4=(3ℓ+r)/(ℓ²(ℓ+r)³). Then Q_{ℓ,r}(x)≥1_{x≥0} for every real x; equivalently, Q_{ℓ,r}(x)≥0 for all x and Q_{ℓ,r}(x)≥1 for all x≥0. Consequently Q_{ℓ,r}(x−δ)≥1_{x≥δ} for every real δ. In the special case ℓ=r=s, Q_s(x)=1+3x/(4s)−x²/s²−x³/(4s³)+x⁴/(2s⁴). Also q4>0 always, and q3≤0 whenever ℓ≤(1+√3)r/2, with strict inequality unless equality holds. Moreover, if X is any real random variable with E X=0 and finite fourth moment, then for every real δ, E[Q_{ℓ,r}(X−δ)]=∑_{i=0}^4 (−δ)^i q_i +(q2−3δq3+6δ²q4)E[X²]+(q3−4δq4)E[X³]+q4 E[X⁴].
proof_sketch: The coefficients are chosen so that Q has a double root at −ℓ, Q(0)=1, and Q−1 has a double root at r. Expanding these interpolation/tangency conditions gives the displayed q_i. The key proof is by factorization: Q_{ℓ,r}(x)=[(ℓ+x)²((ℓ+r)³−2(ℓ²+3ℓr+r²)x+(3ℓ+r)x²)]/[ℓ²(ℓ+r)³]. The denominator and square are nonnegative; the remaining quadratic has minimum at x=(ℓ²+3ℓr+r²)/(3ℓ+r), where its value is [(3ℓ+r)(ℓ+r)³−(ℓ²+3ℓr+r²)²]/(3ℓ+r)=(2ℓ⁴+4ℓ³r+ℓ²r²)/(3ℓ+r)≥0. Hence Q≥0 on R. Similarly, Q_{ℓ,r}(x)−1=[x(4ℓ²+2ℓr+(3ℓ+r)x)(x−r)²]/[ℓ²(ℓ+r)³], which is ≥0 for x≥0, proving Q≥1 on the half-line. The shifted majorization is immediate by replacing x by x−δ. The expectation identity follows by expanding E[(X−δ)^i] for i≤4 and using E X=0; the E[X²], E[X³], and E[X⁴] coefficients collect to q2−3δq3+6δ²q4, q3−4δq4, and q4 respectively. This is the paper's main reusable dual-polynomial device: tail probabilities are bounded by E Q, which is then reduced to moment estimates.

statement: [arXiv:1804.01529 Theorem 2.1] Let X be a non-degenerate real-valued random variable with E X=0, E[X²]=σ²>0, and E[X³]≥0. If E[X⁴]≤cσ⁴ for some c>0, then Pr[X≥0]≤1−1/(2c). The bound is sharp: for any a>0 and any p0<1/2, the distribution taking values −a,0,a with probabilities p0,1−2p0,p0 has E[X³]=0, E[X⁴]=cσ⁴ with c=1/(2p0), and Pr[X≥0]=1−1/(2c).
proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 2.2 with the symmetric choice ℓ=r=√c σ. Then Q(x)=1+3x/(4√c σ)−x²/(cσ²)−x³/(4c^{3/2}σ³)+x⁴/(2c²σ⁴) majorizes 1_{x≥0}. Therefore Pr[X≥0]≤E Q(X). Since E X=0, the linear term vanishes. The quadratic term contributes −σ²/(cσ²)=−1/c. The cubic coefficient is negative, and the assumption E[X³]≥0 makes the cubic contribution nonpositive, so it can be discarded in an upper bound. The fourth-moment hypothesis gives x⁴ contribution at most (1/(2c²σ⁴))·cσ⁴=1/(2c). Thus E Q(X)≤1−1/c+1/(2c)=1−1/(2c). For sharpness, compute the moments of the three-point symmetric distribution: σ²=2p0 a², E[X³]=0, E[X⁴]=2p0 a⁴, hence c=E[X⁴]/σ⁴=1/(2p0), while Pr[X≥0]=1−p0=1−1/(2c).

statement: [arXiv:1804.01529 Lemma 3.1] Let X1,…,Xn be a 4-wise independent collection of real random variables, meaning every subcollection of at most four variables is mutually independent. Assume E[Xi]=0 and |Xi|≤1 almost surely for each i. Put X=∑_{i=1}^n Xi. If E[X³]≥0, then Pr[X≥4/25]≤5/6.
proof_sketch: Let σ²=E[X²]. If σ=0 the claim is trivial. By 4-wise independence and centering, E[X⁴]=∑E[Xi⁴]+6∑_{i<j}E[Xi²]E[Xj²]=3(∑E[Xi²])²+∑(E[Xi⁴]−3E[Xi²]²)≤3σ⁴+σ², since |Xi|≤1 gives E[Xi⁴]≤E[Xi²]. Use Lemma 2.2 on Q_{ℓ,r}(X−δ) with δ=4/25. For all parameter choices used, q4>0 and q3−4δq4<0; hence the hypothesis E[X³]≥0 allows the cubic term in the shifted expectation expansion to be dropped. Thus E Q_{ℓ,r}(X−δ)≤∑(−δ)^i q_i +(q2−3δq3+6δ²q4)σ²+q4(3σ⁴+σ²). It remains to choose ℓ,r as functions of σ so this is ≤5/6. For σ>5/4 take ℓ=r=√3 σ, giving E Q≤5/6+[2δ⁴+√3δ³σ+2σ²−6√3δσ³]/(36σ⁴), and with δ=4/25 the bracket is ≤0 using σ^{-1}<4/5. For 0.68≤σ≤1.25 take ℓ=2σ, r=5σ/2; the upper bound becomes 835/972−226δ σ^{-1}/729+(68−207δ²)σ^{-2}/2916+11δ³σ^{-3}/243+17δ⁴σ^{-4}/729. As a function of t=σ^{-1} this is convex, and direct endpoint checks at σ=0.68 and 1.25 put it below 5/6. For 0.5≤σ≤0.68 take ℓ=15σ/7, r=3σ; the analogous bound is 256957/291600−10633δ σ^{-1}/32400+(26411−128625δ²)σ^{-2}/1749600+7889δ³σ^{-3}/194400+26411δ⁴σ^{-4}/1749600, again convex in σ^{-1} and below 5/6 at the endpoints. For 0≤σ≤0.5 take fixed ℓ=1,r=2, obtaining E Q≤62573/78125−59σ²/3375+5σ⁴/9≤5/6. Since Pr[X≥δ]≤E Q_{ℓ,r}(X−δ), these cases prove the lemma.

statement: [arXiv:1703.05152 Theorem 1.1] Let n≥1, let μ1≥...≥μn>0 with ∑_{i=1}^n μ_i=1, and let X1,...,Xn be independent non-negative random variables with E X_i=1 for every i. Put Z=∑_{i=1}^n μ_i X_i, M=μ1, let δ>0, and set T=1+δ. Assume that the following Samuels lower bound is valid for all such data: P(Z<T) ≥ min_{1≤i≤n} Π_{j=1}^i (1 − μ_j/(T − ∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k)), where the tail sum is interpreted as 0 when i=n. Then the following Feige lower bound is valid for all such data: P(Z<T) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e).
proof_sketch: The proof is local: fix one admissible choice and assume its Samuels product bound. Write σ_i=∑_{k=1}^i μ_k, so T−∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k=σ_i+δ. Choose i attaining the minimum in Samuels' bound. Introduce Φ(a,ρ)=a^{-1} log(1−a/(1+ρ)) for a>0 and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ). For j≤i, with a_j=μ_j/σ_i and ρ=δ/σ_i, one has log(1−μ_j/(σ_i+δ))=a_j Φ(a_j,ρ). Hence log P(Z<T) is at least ∑_{j≤i} a_j Φ(a_j,ρ). The key monotonicity input is that Φ is strictly decreasing in its first variable; this follows by writing Φ(a,ρ)=(1/(1+ρ))φ(a/(1+ρ)), where φ(s)=log(1−s)/s=−∑_{k≥0}s^k/(k+1), a strictly decreasing power series on [0,1). Since a_j≤M/σ_i and ∑ a_j=1, the weighted average is at least Φ(M/σ_i,δ/σ_i). Now σ_i∈[M,1]. Put t=M/σ_i and α=δ/M; then the lower bound is at least min_{t∈[M,1]} Φ(t,αt). Lemma 3.4 says t↦Φ(t,αt) is concave, so its minimum over the interval is attained at an endpoint: min(Φ(1,δ/M), Φ(M,δ)). The first endpoint equals log(δ/(δ+M)); exponentiating gives the intermediate product lower bound min(δ/(δ+M), (1−M/(1+δ))^{1/M}). Finally Lemma 3.3 gives exp(Φ(M,δ))≥min(δ/(δ+M),1/e), yielding P(Z<T)≥min(δ/(δ+M),1/e). Thus the argument actually proves the deterministic product estimate min_i Π_{j≤i}(1−μ_j/(σ_i+δ))≥min(δ/(δ+M),1/e), and Samuels' bound transfers it to the probability.

statement: [arXiv:1703.05152 Lemma 3.3] Define Φ:[0,1]×(0,∞)→R by Φ(μ,ρ)=μ^{-1} log(1−μ/(1+ρ)) for μ∈(0,1], and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ), with log denoting the natural logarithm. Then for every μ∈[0,1] and every ρ>0, Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ log(min(ρ/(μ+ρ), 1/e)).
proof_sketch: The boundary cases are immediate: for μ=0, Φ=−1/(1+ρ)>−1=log(1/e); for μ=1, Φ=log(ρ/(1+ρ)), which is at least the logarithm of the displayed minimum. Fix 0<μ<1. First note that ∂Φ/∂ρ = 1/((1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ))>0, so Φ increases with ρ. Split at ρ0=μ/(e−1), the point where ρ/(μ+ρ)=1/e. If ρ≥ρ0, the desired lower bound is Φ≥−1. By monotonicity in ρ it suffices to prove Φ(μ,ρ0)≥−1, i.e. 1−μ/(1+μ/(e−1))≥e^{−μ}. This is Lemma 3.1: for 0≤t≤1, 1−t/(1+t/(e−1))≥e^{−t}. The paper proves it by setting f(t)=(1+t/(e−1))(1−e^{−t})−t, computing f(0)=f(1)=0 and f''(t)=(e^{−t}/(e−1))(3−e−t), so f is first convex then concave and stays nonnegative. If 0<ρ≤ρ0, the desired lower bound is Φ(μ,ρ)≥log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). Define g(ρ)=Φ(μ,ρ)−log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). A direct derivative computation gives g'(ρ)=−(1−μ)((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2)/(ρ(1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ)(μ+ρ)). Since ρ≤μ/(e−1)<μ, the numerator factor ((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2) is positive, so g'<0 on (0,ρ0]. Hence g(ρ)≥g(ρ0). But g(ρ0)=Φ(μ,ρ0)+1≥0 by the previous case, completing the proof.

statement: [arXiv:1703.05152 Lemma 3.4] For every α>0, define h_α:[0,1]→R by h_α(0)=−1 and h_α(t)=t^{-1} log(1−t/(1+αt)) for 0<t≤1; equivalently, h_α(t)=Φ(t,αt) for t>0, with the value at 0 understood by continuous extension. Then h_α is concave on [0,1].
proof_sketch: The extension at 0 is continuous because log(1−t/(1+αt))=−t+O(t^2). The function is smooth on (0,1), so it is enough to prove h_α''(t)≤0 there. Rather than inspect h_α'' directly, the proof multiplies by t^3 and sets η(t)=t^3 h_α''(t). Direct differentiation gives η(t)= t(2+(6α−3)t+4(α^2−α)t^2)/((1−t+αt)^2(1+αt)^2) + 2 log(1−t/(1+αt)). Differentiating this expression produces the decisive factorization η'(t)=−t^2(1+3(1−2α+2(α−α^2)t)^2)/(2(1−t+αt)^3(1+αt)^3). For α>0 and 0<t<1 both denominator factors are positive, and the numerator is strictly positive, so η'(t)<0. Since lim_{s↓0} η(s)=0, it follows that η(t)<0 for every t∈(0,1), hence h_α''(t)<0 on (0,1). Continuity at the endpoints then gives concavity on the whole interval. A frequently used corollary is that for any subinterval [a,b]⊆[0,1], min_{t∈[a,b]} h_α(t)=min(h_α(a),h_α(b)).

statement: [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Lemma 4 (reduce support)] Let X_1,...,X_n be independent nonnegative random variables with finite supports, let X=Σ_j X_j and μ=E[X], and fix δ>0. Suppose that for some index i, X_i has support {v_1,...,v_k} with k≥3. Then X_i can be replaced by a random variable X_i' that is independent of {X_j:j≠i}, has E[X_i']=E[X_i], and has support consisting of at most two values from {v_1,...,v_k}, in such a way that, writing X'=X_i'+Σ_{j≠i} X_j, one has E[X']=μ and Pr[X'<μ+δ]≤Pr[X<μ+δ].
proof_sketch: For each support point v_j of X_i, let q_j be the conditional probability of the target lower-tail event when X_i is fixed to v_j: q_j=Pr[Σ_{ℓ≠i} X_ℓ+v_j<μ+δ]. A replacement distribution on the same support is specified by probabilities p_j. Preserving total mass and mean imposes the two linear constraints Σ_j p_j=1 and Σ_j p_j v_j=E[X_i], with p_j≥0. The resulting lower-tail probability is the linear objective Σ_j q_j p_j. The original distribution of X_i is feasible, so the minimum of this linear program is at most the original lower-tail probability. A basic optimal solution of an LP with two equality constraints has at most two positive p_j's (apart from degeneracy, still no more than two are needed). Taking X_i' with this basic optimal distribution gives the claim. The same LP idea is later reused in the paper with the objective Pr[sum<mean] instead of Pr[sum<mean+δ], yielding the modified reduce-support operation.

statement: [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Proposition 8] Let X_1,...,X_n be independent random variables. For each i, let μ_i=E[X_i], assume μ_1≥μ_2≥...≥μ_n, and assume X_i takes value μ_i+s_i with probability μ_i/(μ_i+s_i) and value 0 otherwise, where s_i≥0, with the evident degenerate interpretation when μ_i=s_i=0. Let X=Σ_i X_i, μ=Σ_i μ_i, and s=Σ_i s_i. Then Pr[X<μ−μ_n+s]≥s/(μ_1+s).
proof_sketch: The maximum possible value of X is μ+s=Σ_i(μ_i+s_i). If some variable with s_i>0 comes up 0, the sum loses μ_i+s_i, which is strictly larger than μ_n because μ_i≥μ_n and s_i>0; hence X<μ+s−μ_n. Variables with s_i=0 are at their upper value almost surely. Therefore the event X≥μ+s−μ_n can occur only when every variable with positive surplus takes its upper value, so Pr[X≥μ+s−μ_n]≤Π_i μ_i/(μ_i+s_i). Since μ_i≤μ_1 and a/(a+t) is increasing in a for t≥0, each factor is at most μ_1/(μ_1+s_i). For fixed total surplus s, Π_i μ_1/(μ_1+s_i)≤μ_1/(μ_1+s); equivalently, combining two surpluses x,y cannot decrease the product because (μ_1/(μ_1+x))(μ_1/(μ_1+y))≤μ_1/(μ_1+x+y). Thus Pr[X≥μ+s−μ_n]≤μ_1/(μ_1+s), and taking complements gives Pr[X<μ−μ_n+s]≥1−μ_1/(μ_1+s)=s/(μ_1+s).

statement: [arXiv:www_wisdom_weizmann_ac_il_6853801a2670 Lemma 10 (stage-2 modified merge bound)] Consider the following Stage 2 process on a finite collection of independent nonconstant two-point random variables. At any time, variable X_i has mean μ_i>0 and takes values 0 and μ_i+s_i, where s_i≥0 is its surplus. Let X=Σ_i X_i, μ=Σ_i μ_i, and let μ_1=max_i μ_i. Initially all means lie in [μ_1/2, μ_1], and the total surplus is at least the initial minimum mean; in particular, for α=1/3, the initial value of s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i is greater than α μ_1. A modified merge step chooses the two variables of currently smallest means, replaces them by a variable distributed as their sum, applies the support-reduction LP with objective Pr[new total < new mean] to reduce this replacement to at most two support points without increasing that exact lower-tail probability, and then aligns the resulting two-point variable with 0 by subtracting its lower support point from that variable and from the total mean, or removes it if it is constant. After each step redefine μ_1=max_i μ_i and s'=Σ_{i: μ_i≥μ_1/2} s_i. Run modified merge steps until either only one nonconstant variable remains or s'≤α μ_1. For α=1/3, if r is the stopping time, then either Pr[X^r<μ^r]≥1/13, or, if the stopping time was reached by a last modified merge, the collection one step before stopping satisfies Pr[X^{r−1}<μ^{r−1}]≥1/13.
proof_sketch: Each modified merge preserves or decreases the exact lower-tail probability Pr[X<μ]: the LP is chosen to minimize that probability at the same mean, and aligning with 0 or removing a constant shifts both X and μ by the same deterministic amount. The process maintains that at most one variable has mean below μ_1/2: when two smallest means are merged, all unmerged variables have mean at least half of any new maximum. Set β=1/8. If the process stops with one variable while s'>αμ_1, then Pr[X<μ]=Pr[this variable is 0]=s'/(μ_1+s')>α/(1+α). If it stops with βμ_1≤s'≤αμ_1, write s'=γμ_1. Apply Proposition 8 to the variables with mean at least μ_1/2: with probability at least γ/(1+γ), their partial sum is below its mean by (1/2−γ)μ_1. The only possible spoiler is the single low-mean variable. If its mean is at most s', Markov bounds the probability that it fills this gap by at most 2γ, giving Pr[X<μ]≥(γ−2γ^2)/(1+γ); if its mean is larger than s', its zero atom gives an even larger bound. Over γ∈[β,α], this is minimized at an endpoint and is at least 1/12 for α=1/3, β=1/8. It remains to handle stopping with s'<βμ_1. Look one step earlier, normalize the old maximum mean to 1, and let the merged variables have means μ_i≥μ_j. Before the last merge, s'≥α; surplus outside the merged pair is at most β times the new maximum. If μ_j<1/2, then only X_i among the pair counted toward s' before the merge, so s_i≥α−β(new maximum). The event X_i=0, together with X_j failing to compensate the deficit after allowing the outside surplus, forces X<μ; optimizing over the endpoint possibilities μ_i=1/2 or 1 yields the bounds ((α−β)/(1/2+α−β))*((1/2−β)/(1−β)) and ((α−3β/2)/(1+α−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)). If μ_j≥1/2, both merged variables counted, and s_i+s_j≥α−β(μ_i+μ_j)≥α−2β. If their pair sum is below (1−β)(μ_i+μ_j), the outside variables cannot restore the total to μ. Depending on whether a zero of either variable suffices, exactly one zero suffices, or both zeros are needed, one obtains the bounds (α−2β)/(1+α−2β), ((1/2−β)/(1−β))^2, and ((1/2−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2))*((1−3β/2)/(3/2−3β/2)); the one-zero-only subcases are dominated. Substituting α=1/3 and β=1/8 makes every displayed bound at least 1/13, with (α−2β)/(1+α−2β)=1/13.

statement: [arXiv:1011.2803 Complement-pair observation (Introduction)] Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with total sum ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least 2^{n-1} subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0.
proof_sketch: Partition the power set of [n] into the 2^{n-1} unordered complementary pairs {S,[n]\S}. For each pair, the two subset sums add to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Hence the two sums cannot both be negative, so every complementary pair contributes at least one subset with nonnegative sum. This gives at least one nonnegative subset from each of 2^{n-1} pairs. Under the usual convention that the empty sum is 0, the pair {∅,[n]} may contribute two subsets, but the lower bound needed in the paper is 2^{n-1}. For the weighted Bernoulli p=1/2 endpoint, the same pairing says that for every subset S, the weights of S and its complement add to 1, so at least one has weight at least 1/2.

statement: [arXiv:1011.2803 Baranyai partition corollary / multiple-of-k case] Let n and k be positive integers with k dividing n. Let x_1,...,x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least C(n-1,k-1) of the k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] satisfy ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, in the notation A(n,k) for the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums over all n-tuples of real numbers with nonnegative total sum, one has A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) whenever k divides n.
proof_sketch: Use Baranyai's partition theorem: when k|n, the family [n]^{(k)} of all k-subsets can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is [n]. The number of parallel classes is C(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)C(n,k) = C(n-1,k-1). In any one parallel class, the sums of the n/k disjoint k-sets add exactly to the total sum ∑_i x_i, which is nonnegative. Therefore not all k-set sums in that class can be negative; each class contains at least one nonnegative k-sum. Since the classes partition [n]^{(k)}, the nonnegative k-sets found in distinct classes are distinct, giving at least C(n-1,k-1) of them. The matching upper-bound example is x_1=n-1 and x_2=...=x_n=-1: for n≥k, exactly the k-subsets containing x_1 have nonnegative sum, and there are C(n-1,k-1) such subsets.

statement: [arXiv:1011.2803 Theorem 3 (main bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture; typo-corrected statement)] Let k≥2 and n be positive integers, and let log denote the natural logarithm. If n > k(4e log k)^k, then every collection x_1,...,x_n of n real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0 has at least C(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets S ⊆ [n] with ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. Equivalently, A(n,k)=C(n-1,k-1) for all n > k(4e log k)^k. The paper's displayed theorem has an evident typo in the first quantifier; this is the form stated in the abstract and proved by the argument.
proof_sketch: Order x_1≥...≥x_n. For any ordered finite set Y={y_1≥...≥y_N} with nonnegative total, the basic inequality y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} follows from j y_1+(N-j)y_{j+1} ≥ ∑Y ≥0. Call an element central in Y if every k-subset of Y containing it has nonnegative sum. If x_1 is central in X, the C(n-1,k-1) k-sets containing x_1 finish the proof. Otherwise, since x_1 is the largest element, x_1 plus the k−1 smallest elements has negative sum; delete these k elements. The remaining set has nonnegative total. Iterate: X_1=X; if the largest remaining element x_i is not central in X_i, delete it and the k−1 smallest elements of X_i to form X_{i+1}. If at stage p+1 the largest element x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, whose size is n−kp, then for every (k−1)-subset T of X_{p+1}\{x_{p+1}} and every r≤p+1, the set T∪{x_r} is nonnegative, because x_r≥x_{p+1}. These families are disjoint, so there are at least (p+1)C(n−kp−1,k−1) nonnegative k-sets. For 1≤p≤n/(2k), the paper proves this is already larger than C(n−1,k−1): using C(n−kp−1,k−1) ≥ (n−k(p+1))^{k−1}/(k−1)! and C(n−1,k−1) ≤ n^{k−1}/(k−1)!, it suffices to show (p+1)(n−k(p+1))^{k−1}>n^{k−1}. If p<n/k^2, lower-bound (n−a)^{k−1} by its first term minus second term, with a=k(p+1), valid here once n>k^3; the condition reduces to n/[k(k−1)]>p+2+1/p, implied by p<n/k^2 and n>3k^2(k−1). If n/k^2≤p<n/(2k), use p+1≥n/k^2 and, with harmless integer endpoint rounding, n−k(p+1)≥n/2 to get a lower bound n^k/(2^{k−1}k^2)>n^{k−1} when n>2^{k−1}k^2. Hence, unless the theorem is already proved, no central element appears in the first about n/(2k) iterations. In that case, after q=n/(2k) deletions the current set has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Apply the basic inequality to it with j=n/(2 log k) to get x_q ≥ −(log k−1)x_{q+j}. Thus any k-set formed by choosing a=k/log k elements from the first block {x_1,...,x_q} and b=k−a elements from the next block of length j has nonnegative sum, since b=a(log k−1) and each chosen first-block element is at least x_q while each chosen second-block element is at least x_{q+j}. The number of such k-sets is C(n/(2k), k/log k) C(n/(2 log k), k−k/log k). The paper lower-bounds this by n^k/k! · (1/(4k))^{k/log k}(1/(4 log k))^{k−k/log k} > n^k/[k!(4e log k)^k], using k^{k/log k}=e^k and routine binomial estimates. If n>k(4e log k)^k, this exceeds n^{k−1}/(k−1)! ≥ C(n−1,k−1), completing the proof. Floor/ceiling issues in quantities such as n/(2k) and k/log k are suppressed in the paper and are handled by routine rounding.

statement: Counterexamples: for p in (1/3,1/2), m=3 and w_i=1/3 gives Pr[sum w_i v_i >= p] = p^2(3-2p) < p; for p in (1/2,1), m=2 and w_i=1/2 gives probability p^2 < p.
Endpoint p=1/2 follows by pairing every subset A with its complement A^c under the uniform measure; at least one of w(A), w(A^c) is >= 1/2.

Endpoint p=1/3 follows by a three-colour coupling: for each coordinate choose one of three colours uniformly, define three Bernoulli(1/3) vectors by colour indicators, and use that the three weighted sums add to 1.
Endpoints p=0 and p=1 are trivial.

value: The only missing part of the candidate classification is sufficiency for every 0 < p < 1/3.
Any proof must be sharp at dictator weights and must handle threshold jumps at subset sums.
Prove p < 1/3 sufficiency, preferably through either the fractional-cover down-set theorem or the strict-threshold lemma Pr_p[w(S) > p] >= p.

note: Candidate answer is [0,1/3] union {1/2,1}. Easy necessity and endpoints are ready for a partial write-up; all effort should focus on proving p < 1/3. Two promising equivalent/stronger targets are: a fractional-cover theorem for down-sets of p-biased measure > 1-p, and the strict-threshold lemma Pr_p[w(S) > p] >= p for p < 1/3.

statement: Partial classification: the inequality holds at p=0, p=1/3, p=1/2, and p=1, and fails for every p in (1/3,1/2) union (1/2,1).
Partial classification: the inequality holds at p=0, p=1/3, p=1/2, and p=1, and fails for every p in (1/3,1/2) union (1/2,1).
proof_excerpt: For p=½ the proof pairs each random subset A with its complement, noting that w(A)+w(Aᶜ)=1 so in every pair one side has weight ≥½, and by the uniformity of A each such event has probability at least ½. For p=1/3 a three-colour coupling creates three independent Bernoulli(1/3) vectors whose weighted sums S₀+S₁+S₂=1, so by pigeonhole at least one Sᵣ≥1/3 and symmetry forces each to occur with probability ≥1/3. Sharpness is then settled by the simplest equal‐weight counterexamples (m=3 when p∈(1/3,1/2) and m=2 when p∈(1/2,1)) showing the inequality strictly fails elsewhere.
problem_solved:
The proof establishes the following partial classification.

For every positive integer \(m\), every choice of nonnegative weights \(w_1,\dots,w_m\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_1,\dots,v_m\), the inequality
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
holds for
\[
p\in\left\{0,\frac13,\frac12,1\right\}.
\]

It also proves failure of the universal inequality for every
\[
p\in\left(\frac13,\frac12\right)
\]
using \(m=3\) and \(w_1=w_2=w_3=\frac13\), where
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2(3-2p)<p.
\]

It further proves failure for every
\[
p\in\left(\frac12,1\right)
\]
using \(m=2\) and \(w_1=w_2=\frac12\), where
\[
\Pr[S\ge p]=p^2<p.
\]

The proof does not address the interval \(0<p<\frac13\).

statement: For a fixed increasing family F, if mu_t(F)=t then d/dt mu_t(F) >= 1; more generally t I_t(F) >= mu_t(F) log_t(mu_t(F)).
proof_sketch: Proved by induction on the cube using sections and a two-point convexity inequality for Phi(x)=x log_t x.

statement: A strict counterexample cannot arise by continuous downward crossing between subset-sum thresholds; any strict counterexample for p<1/3 yields a boundary counterexample at some subset-sum a<=p.
proof_sketch: On intervals where F_t is fixed, the ratio log(mu_t(F))/log(t) is nonincreasing, so once mu_t(F)>=t holds it persists to the right.

statement: The strict lemma holds for all weight vectors of support size at most 5 and all 0<p<1/3.
proof_sketch: If all weights are <=p, then for n=4 every pair is winning; for n=5 either every pair is winning or at least six pairs are winning, giving an explicit binomial lower bound exceeding p.

statement: The original non-strict statement holds for p=1/k, k>=3.
proof_sketch: Represent Bernoulli(1/k) variables by uniform residues mod k and partition the product space into diagonal cyclic orbits; each orbit has average weighted sum 1/k, so at least one point per orbit is >=1/k.

approach: Orbit proof for rational p=a/b
reason: A length-a cyclic moving average need not have at least a positions above its average; an explicit p=2/7 orbit has only one good shift.

approach: Charge critical boundary sets to winning supersets
reason: At p=1/4 with four weights 1/4, the critical singleton mass exceeds the total strict winning mass, so no direct injection/charging can work.

approach: Local merging of small weights
reason: Conditioning on the residual selected weight shows that merging can either increase or decrease the overflow probability depending on the residual interval.

statement: For a fixed family G, G admits a probability distribution with all coordinate marginals at least p iff for every nonnegative weight vector w summing to 1 there is A in G with w(A) >= p.
proof_sketch: Identify distributions on G with points in conv{1_A:A in G}; separate this convex hull from [p,infinity)^m. The separating vector must be nonnegative and normalizes to the desired weight vector.

statement: FC(p) is equivalent to the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality for the same p.
proof_sketch: FC(p) applied to the bad down-set {A:w(A)<p} gives a contradiction by averaging w(A). Conversely, if the Bernoulli inequality holds and a down-set G of measure >1-p has no cover, separation gives w with G subset {A:w(A)<p}, contradicting the inequality.

statement: For p=1/k, the Bernoulli inequality Pr[sum_i w_i v_i >= p] >= p holds for all nonnegative weights summing to 1.
proof_sketch: Represent Bernoulli(1/k) variables by uniform elements of Z/kZ and partition the product space into diagonal cyclic orbits. On every orbit the average weighted sum is 1/k, so at least one of the k points is good.

statement: For 1/3 < p < 1/2, FC(p) fails on m=3 with G={A:|A|<=1}; for p>1/2 it already fails on m=2 with G={A:|A|<=1}.
proof_sketch: For m=3, mu_p(G)=(1-p)^2(1+2p)>1-p iff p<1/2, but any distribution on G has expected size at most 1, so cannot give all three marginals >1/3. The m=2 case is analogous for p>1/2.

statement: If p<=1/3, all weights are <p, and every pair of positive weights has sum at least p, then the Bernoulli inequality holds.
proof_sketch: Then two successes always certify sum at least p, so the good probability is at least Pr[Bin(s,p)>=2]. For the relevant support sizes this binomial tail is at least p.

statement: Any counterexample for p<=1/3 must have all weights <p, support size at least floor(1/p)+2, and some pair of weights with sum <p.
proof_sketch: A weight >=p gives the result immediately. If the support is at most floor(1/p)+1, then the two smallest weights already sum to at least p, reducing to the pairwise-heavy lemma.

approach: Section induction
reason: The easy case mu_p(G_1)>1-p closes, but the remaining case requires a two-threshold gluing lemma for conv(G_0) and conv(G_1) that the measure hypothesis alone does not immediately supply.

approach: Naive stronger cover for G_0
reason: Mixing with coordinate m at marginal p would require something like a p/(1-p)-cover of G_0 when G_1 contributes little, which is false in general.

approach: Orbit-wise rational-p extension
reason: The needed finite hypergeometric statement is false; for n=10,r=3 and weights seven times 1/7 plus three zeros, a uniform 3-subset reaches 3/10 with probability 35/120<3/10.

approach: Pairwise smoothing or merging weights
reason: Merging/smoothing two weights changes the tail in opposite directions depending on the rest-sum window, so no monotonicity adequate for a global proof emerged.

statement: Exact section-gluing dual reduction: to prove the fractional-cover theorem FC(p) by induction, it is enough to prove a two-threshold knapsack inequality. Namely, for 0<p≤1/3, weights u_i≥0 with sum_i u_i=1, and thresholds 0≤b≤a with (1-p)a+p b<p, it would suffice to show (1-p) mu_p({A:u(A)≤a}) + p mu_p({A:u(A)≤b}) ≤ 1-p.
proof_sketch:
Let G be a down-set with sections G0 and G1, where G1⊂G0. To glue a cover using coordinate n with marginal r≥p, one needs x0 in conv(G0), x1 in conv(G1), and (1-r)x0+r x1≥p 1. If no such triple exists, separating from the orthant gives a nonnegative vector u. Put a=max_{A in G0} u(A), b=max_{A in G1} u(A). Since b≤a, maximizing (1-r)a+r b over r∈[p,1] occurs at r=p, so separation gives (1-p)a+p b

1-p.

approach: Pointwise continuous diagonal-orbit or circle-window lemma for arbitrary p≤1/3.
reason: For p=2/7, four equal weights 1/4 placed at residues 0,1,3,5 modulo 7 have only one of seven cyclic length-2 windows of mass at least 2/7; equivalently, arcs of length 2/7 on the circle can have superlevel set of length 1/7, so orbit-wise pointwise averaging cannot prove the theorem.

value: Prove the two-threshold knapsack inequality arising from section gluing.
Control threshold jumps/critical antichains in the strict inequality mu_p({w(A)>p})≥p for p<1/3.
Find a polynomial or moment-dual lower bound that exploits the Bernoulli moment structure rather than only support bounds.
Establish p<1/3 sufficiency, preferably by proving the two-threshold knapsack inequality: if (1-p)a+p b<p, then (1-p)P[u(A)≤a]+pP[u(A)≤b]≤1-p.

note: The main frontier is no longer just the one-threshold boundary inequality; a sharper two-threshold inequality naturally arises from section gluing and would imply the full FC(p) theorem. Run one solver on that exact induction/dual inequality and one solver on an orthogonal moment-polynomial proof attempt.

statement: In the section setup G1 subset G0, failure of the gluing condition yields nonnegative weights u summing to 1 and thresholds 0 <= b <= a with (1-p)a + p b < p such that G0 is contained in {u <= a} and G1 is contained in {u <= b}.
proof_sketch: The achievable old-coordinate marginals equal (1-p)conv(G0) + p conv(G1). Separating this convex set from [p,infinity)^n gives a nonnegative separator u; its maxima on G0 and G1 are a and b.

statement: The proposed two-threshold inequality is equivalent to the one-threshold submean lemma: for all weights w summing to 1 and all tau < p, Pr_p[sum w_i v_i > tau] >= p.
proof_sketch: Set delta = a-b and add an independent Bernoulli(p) coordinate of weight delta. Then (1-p)Pr[X <= a] + p Pr[X <= b] = Pr[X + delta xi <= a]; after normalization the threshold is tau = a/(1+delta) < p.

statement: The submean lemma is equivalent to the original Bernoulli inequality Pr_p[sum w_i v_i >= p] >= p.
proof_sketch: The original inequality implies the submean version because {S >= p} is contained in {S > tau}. Conversely let tau increase to p and use continuity from above.

statement: The two-threshold inequality holds for reciprocal parameters p = 1/k.
proof_sketch: Use the cyclic orbit proof to get Pr[weighted sum >= 1/k] >= 1/k for the augmented normalized weights; since the reduced threshold tau is below 1/k, the target follows.

statement: The two-threshold inequality holds whenever the augmented support size is at most 5.
proof_sketch: After augmentation reduce to proving Pr[Z > tau] >= p with tau < p. If no coordinate exceeds tau, supports 4 and 5 are handled by pair-counting: all pairs win in the small-threshold cases, and for support 5 with tau >= 1/4 at least six pairs and all triples win.

statement: The two-threshold inequality holds in the augmented pairwise-heavy case, where every pair of positive augmented weights has sum greater than the augmented threshold.
proof_sketch: Then every outcome with at least two successes is good. The augmented support is greater than 1/p, and the binomial tail Pr[Bin(N,p) >= 2] is at least p for p <= 1/3.

statement: The two-threshold inequality holds under the variance-slack condition p - (1-p)a - p b >= p sqrt(sum u_i^2 + (a-b)^2).
proof_sketch: Apply Cantelli's inequality to the augmented normalized weighted Bernoulli sum; the stated condition is exactly what makes the lower-tail bound at most 1-p.

approach: Direct coordinate induction
reason: After conditioning on a coordinate, the normalized threshold in the zero branch can exceed p, so the induction hypothesis does not apply.

approach: Weight merging/compression
reason: Splitting two weights decreases the lower tail in one residual interval and increases it in another, so no monotone compression direction emerges.

approach: Boundary continuity
reason: The non-strict boundary version is false, e.g. p=1/3 with five equal weights, a=2/5, b=1/5, so jumps at subset-sum thresholds must be controlled.

statement: For any p<r<=1-p, the cubic polynomial L_r(x)=x(x+p)(r-x)/M_3(r) is a valid lower bound for 1_{x>0} on [-p,1-p], giving Pr[X>0] >= pq((r-p)A2-(1-2p)A3)/M_3(r).
proof_sketch: The factor x(x+p) makes the polynomial nonpositive on [-p,0], and the root at r makes it nonpositive again on [r,1-p]; scaling by its positive maximum gives the certificate.

statement: For any 0<r<=s<=1-p, the quartic polynomial x(x+p)(r-x)(s-x), scaled by its positive maximum on [0,1-p], gives a valid lower-polynomial certificate with expectation mu4+(p-r-s)mu3+(rs-p(r+s))mu2.
proof_sketch: The polynomial is nonpositive on the negative interval and is bounded above by its positive maximum on the positive interval, so its scaled expectation lower-bounds Pr[X>0].

statement: A Berry-Esseen diffuse-weight criterion holds: Pr[X>0] >= 1/2 - C_BE * ((p^2+(1-p)^2)/sqrt(p(1-p))) * A3/A2^{3/2}; hence the desired bound follows whenever this error is at most 1/2-p.
proof_sketch: Apply Berry-Esseen to the independent centered summands w_i(v_i-p), using variance p(1-p)A2 and third absolute moment sum p(1-p)(p^2+(1-p)^2)A3.

statement: In the strict-reduced regime 0<w_i<p, sum w_i=1, p<1/3, the inequality Pr[sum w_i v_i>p]>p holds for support size exactly 6.
proof_sketch: Classify losing pairs and triples. All 4-sets are winning; losing pairs/triples are bounded by elementary matching and intersecting-family arguments in four p-ranges, and the resulting binomial-polynomial lower bounds are all strictly larger than p.

approach: Quadratic lower polynomial
reason: The optimal quadratic certificate has expectation at most mu2/(1-p)=pA2, which is far below p in the strict-reduced regime.

approach: Simple quartic complement majorant with two double positive roots
reason: The expectation has competing mu2, mu3, and mu4 terms and does not become sign-definite after substituting the Bernoulli moment formulas.

approach: Quartic lower polynomial with roots -p, 0, r, 1-p as a universal certificate
reason: After substituting moments, the negative mu3 term and the sometimes-negative A4 contribution prevent a uniform inequality from the elementary constraints A3<=pA2 and A4<=p^2A2.

statement: Bootstrap/scaling lemma: Let B(t) denote the assertion that for all nonnegative weights summing to 1 and iid Bernoulli(t) variables, Pr[sum_i w_i v_i >= t] >= t. If B(r) holds and r = k p for some positive integer k, then B(p) holds.
proof_sketch: For each coordinate independently choose a category C_i in {0,1,...,k}, with Pr[C_i=j]=p for j=1,...,k and Pr[C_i=0]=1-r. Let S_j be the total weight of coordinates with C_i=j. Each S_j has the same law as the Bernoulli(p) weighted sum, while T=S_1+...+S_k has the Bernoulli(r) weighted-sum law. If T >= r = k p, then at least one S_j >= p. Thus Pr_r[T>=r] <= Pr[union_j {S_j>=p}] <= k Pr_p[S_1>=p]. Using B(r), k Pr_p[S_1>=p] >= r, hence Pr_p[S_1>=p] >= p.

statement: It suffices to prove B(p) for p in [1/6,1/3] in order to obtain B(p) for every 0 <= p <= 1/3.
proof_sketch: The case p=0 is trivial. If 0<p<1/6, the interval [1/(6p), 1/(3p)] has length at least 1, so it contains an integer k. Then r=kp lies in [1/6,1/3]. If B(r) is known on that base interval, the bootstrap lemma gives B(p).

statement: For the base interval [1/6,1/3], any strict reduced counterexample must have support at least 7.
proof_sketch: The earlier reductions show that a strict p<1/3 counterexample can be taken with all positive weights less than p; tasks t2 and t3 prove the strict statement for supports up to 5 and exactly 6, respectively.

approach: Treating the two-threshold gluing inequality as a simpler induction lemma.
reason: Task t4 showed that after adding one Bernoulli coordinate and normalizing, it is exactly equivalent to the original subthreshold one-threshold statement.

approach: Low-degree universal polynomial certificates through the simple quartic families tried in task t3.
reason: The resulting moment expressions have competing A2, A3, and A4 terms and do not become sign-definite from only the elementary constraints w_i < p.

value: Prove the weighted Bernoulli inequality on the base interval p in [1/6,1/3].
Rule out strict reduced counterexamples with support at least 7 in the base interval.
Find a higher-degree polynomial/moment certificate that exploits the Bernoulli cumulant structure in the base interval.
Prove B(p) for every p in [1/6,1/3]; by the bootstrap lemma this proves the whole missing range 0<p<1/3.

note: The two-threshold inequality is equivalent to the original problem, so stop treating it as an easier induction lemma. A new k-colour scaling lemma reduces the missing range p <= 1/3 to the compact base interval [1/6,1/3]. Next priority: prove the base interval, ideally by ruling out support >= 7 counterexamples or by finding a polynomial certificate valid there.

statement: Bootstrap reduction for the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality: if the inequality holds at r=kp for an integer k, then it holds at p; consequently it is enough to prove the missing range 0<p<=1/3 on the base interval [1/6,1/3].

Bootstrap reduction for the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality: if the inequality holds at r=kp for an integer k, then it holds at p; consequently it is enough to prove the missing range 0<p<=1/3 on the base interval [1/6,1/3].
proof_excerpt: We introduce a categorical coupling by replacing each Bernoulli(p) trial v_i with an independent label C_i∈{0,1,…,k} satisfying Pr(C_i=j)=p for 1≤j≤k and Pr(C_i=0)=1−kp, so that T=∑w_i·1_{C_i≠0} is a weighted sum of Bernoulli(r) variables with r=kp and hence Pr[T≥r]≥r by the hypothesis B(r). Writing T=S_1+⋯+S_k with S_j=∑w_i·1_{C_i=j}, the event T≥r forces some S_j≥p, and by symmetry plus the union bound one deduces Pr[S_1≥p]≥p, which is exactly B(p). Finally, choosing k=⌈1/(6p)⌉ ensures r=kp lies in [1/6,1/3], so the base case on [1/6,1/3] bootstraps to all p≤1/3.
problem_solved:
Let \(B(t)\) denote the assertion that for every \(m\ge 1\), every nonnegative weight vector \(w_1,\ldots,w_m\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((t)\) variables \(v_i\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge t\right]\ge t.
\]
The proof establishes that for every integer \(k\ge1\) and every \(p\in[0,1]\) with \(r=kp\le1\), the implication
\[
B(r)\implies B(p)
\]
holds. It also establishes that if \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[1/6,1/3]\), then \(B(t)\) holds for every \(t\in[0,1/3]\). It does not classify all \(p\) in the original problem.

statement: The fifth and sixth centered moments of X are mu_5 = pq(1-2p)(1-12pq)A5 + 10(pq)^2(1-2p)A2A3 and mu_6 = pq(1-30pq+120p^2q^2)A6 + 15(pq)^2(1-6pq)A2A4 + 10(pq)^2(1-2p)^2A3^2 + 15(pq)^3A2^3.
proof_sketch: Use additivity of cumulants and the standard moment-cumulant identities mu_5 = kappa_5 + 10 kappa_3 kappa_2 and mu_6 = kappa_6 + 15 kappa_4 kappa_2 + 10 kappa_3^2 + 15 kappa_2^3.

statement: For positive roots r_i <= 1-p, the degree-5 and degree-6 product-root polynomials x(x+p)prod_i(r_i-x), scaled by their positive maximum on [0,1-p], give valid lower certificates, with explicit expectations in terms of mu_2 through mu_6.
proof_sketch: The factor x(x+p) makes the polynomial nonpositive on [-p,0]; scaling by the maximum positive value gives L <= 1_{x>0}. Expanding by elementary symmetric functions gives the displayed moment formulas.

statement: The degree-6 square-complement polynomial L_{a,b,c}(x)=1-[(1-x/a)(1-x/b)(1-x/c)]^2 is a valid lower certificate for every a,b,c>0, with expectation explicitly equal to a linear combination of mu_2 through mu_6.
proof_sketch: For x<=0 each factor is at least 1, so L<=0; for x>0 the square is nonnegative, so L<=1. Expanding the square gives the stated expectation.

statement: All degree <=6 lower-polynomial certificates are captured exactly by a Markov-Lukacs/SOS feasibility formulation on [-p,0] and [0,1-p].
proof_sketch: Nonnegativity of -L on [-p,0] and of 1-L on [0,1-p] is equivalent, for degree 6 univariate polynomials, to SOS representations with interval factors (x+p)(-x) and x(1-p-x).

statement: In the strict reduced regime with support exactly 7, the strict inequality Pr[S>p]>p holds for every p in [1/6,1/4].
proof_sketch: For p<=1/5, losing triples are impossible and losing pairs form an intersecting graph, so e<=6. For p in [1/5,1/4], losing 4-sets are impossible, losing pairs have matching number at most 2 so e<=12, and losing triples are intersecting so h<=15. The resulting binomial-polynomial bounds are strictly larger than p.

statement: Product-root degree-(k+2) certificates with all positive roots in (0,1-p] have nonnegative leading variance coefficient only if p<1/(k+1). In particular, degree 5 cannot cover diffuse cases for p>=1/4 and degree 6 cannot cover diffuse cases for p>=1/5.
proof_sketch: The coefficient of mu_2 is e_k - p e_{k-1} = e_k(1 - p sum_i 1/r_i), while sum_i 1/r_i >= k/(1-p).

approach: Universal degree-5/6 product-root closure
reason: The leading mu_2 coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so such certificates cannot handle diffuse moment vectors there.

approach: Automatic sign-definite degree-6 square-complement certificate
reason: After substituting mu_5 and mu_6, the positive odd-moment terms and negative even-moment terms still have competing signs; the elementary constraints A_{j+1}<pA_j do not make the inequality uniform.

approach: Fixed polynomial independent of the weight moments
reason: For equal diffuse weights, X converges to 0 in probability, and any fixed continuous lower polynomial has expectation tending to L(0)<=0, so no fixed bounded-degree polynomial can certify a positive p lower bound.

statement: If B(kp) holds for an integer k with kp <= 1, then B(p) holds.
proof_sketch: Generate Bernoulli(kp) variables and randomly colour each success with one of k colours. Each colour class has iid Bernoulli(p) indicators, and if the total selected weight is at least kp then some colour has weight at least p.

statement: In the strict reduced regime, support n=7 satisfies Pr[sum w_i v_i > p] > p for all 1/6 <= p <= 1/4.
proof_sketch: Count losing layers. For p <= 1/5 there are no losing triples and the losing-pair graph is intersecting; for p <= 1/4 there are no losing 4-sets, losing triples are intersecting, and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2. The resulting binomial-polynomial bounds exceed p.

statement: In the strict reduced regime, support n=8 satisfies Pr[sum w_i v_i > p] > p for all 1/6 <= p <= 1/5.
proof_sketch: There are no losing 4-sets; losing triples are intersecting, and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2. Erdős–Ko–Rado and Erdős–Gallai give d3 <= 21 and d2 <= 13, and the resulting lower bound is larger than p.

statement: For n=7 and 1/4 <= p <= 1/3, the strict inequality holds whenever the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2.
proof_sketch: Losing triples are intersecting, losing 4-sets are 2-intersecting, and the assumed matching bound controls losing pairs. The resulting polynomial lower bound has positive value at p=1/4 and positive derivative on the interval.

statement: For any losing k-uniform family H, its degree sequence must satisfy p sum_{j=1}^s d_(j) + (1-sp)d_(s+1) <= p|H|, where s=floor(1/p).
proof_sketch: Sum the inequalities w(A) <= p over A in H, then minimize the weighted degree sum over weights w_i < p summing to 1 by filling the smallest degrees first.

statement: In a minimal strict counterexample, every mergeable pair x <= y with x+y < p satisfies Pr[p-x<R<=p] < Pr[p-x-y<R<=p-y], where R is the residual sum.
proof_sketch: Compare the bad split instance with the good merged instance. The exact difference of the two success probabilities is pq times the difference of the two residual-window probabilities.

approach: Arbitrary-n matching bounds for losing layers
reason: For large support, losing pairs and triples can have large matchings because many coordinates may be tiny; the small-support contradiction p times leftover-count no longer works.

approach: Colouring with k big colours plus a leftover colour
reason: The deterministic implication only reduces the problem to an upper-tail bound for the leftover colour, and the needed bound Pr_s[weighted sum > s] <= s is false for diffuse equal weights.

approach: Support-7 upper interval by crude layer counts
reason: The bounds d2 <= 21, d3 <= 15, d4 <= 15 are too weak near p=1/3; one needs a sharper tradeoff between many losing pairs and few losing triples/4-sets.

statement: Exact fifth and sixth centered moment formulas for X=sum_i w_i(v_i-p)-p were derived, together with degree-5/6 product-root and square-complement lower-polynomial certificate families.
proof_sketch: Use cumulant additivity for weighted Bernoulli summands and standard moment-cumulant identities; product-root certificates exploit sign on [-p,0] and [0,1-p], while square-complement certificates use L=1-h^2 with h having positive roots.

statement: In the strict reduced regime, support n=7 is ruled out for p in [1/6,1/4], and support n=8 is ruled out for p in [1/6,1/5].
proof_sketch: Count losing layers. Small losing sets cannot be too disjoint because their complements have too few weights, each less than p. Erdős-Gallai and Erdős-Ko-Rado bounds then give explicit p-biased bad-measure polynomials strictly below 1-p.

statement: For n=7 and p in [1/4,1/3], strict counterexamples must have three disjoint losing pairs.
proof_sketch: If the losing-pair graph has matching number at most 2, then losing triples are intersecting and losing 4-sets are 2-intersecting; layer-counting gives success probability strictly greater than p.

statement: For any losing k-uniform family H with degree sequence d_(1)≤...≤d_(n), one has p sum_{j=1}^s d_(j)+(1-sp)d_(s+1)≤p|H|, where s=floor(1/p).
proof_sketch: Sum inequalities w(A)≤p over A in H and minimize the resulting weighted degree sum over 0<w_i<p, sum w_i=1, by filling the smallest degrees first.

proof_sketch: Compare success probabilities before and after merging x and y; their difference is pq times the difference of these two residual-window probabilities.

reason: Their leading variance coefficient is forced nonpositive in the upper base interval, so they cannot handle diffuse weights there.

statement: Finite-support and structural lemmas for strict reduced Bernoulli weighted-sum counterexamples in the base interval.
For every head size k with regular tail, Pr[S >= p] >= G_k - C_BE K(p) rho_k, where G_k is the exact Gaussian-smoothed head probability sum over A subset [k].
proof_sketch: Condition on the head; the tail threshold is p alpha_k - w(A). Apply Berry-Esseen to the centered tail and average over all head configurations.

statement: If p in [1/6,1/3], rho_k <= 1/(64 C_BE), and alpha_k <= sqrt(pq B_k), then Pr[S >= p] > p.
proof_sketch: Given any head outcome, the required centered tail deviation is at most p alpha_k <= sigma_k/3. Berry-Esseen gives at least barPhi(1/3)-1/32, which is larger than p for p <= 1/3.

statement: For one head atom w_1 and a regular tail, Pr[S >= p]-p >= q barPhi(p w_1/sigma_1) - p barPhi(q w_1/sigma_1) - C_BE K(p) rho_1.
proof_sketch: Decompose according to v_1. The difference from p is q Pr[X_tail >= p w_1] - p Pr[X_tail < -q w_1], and Berry-Esseen bounds the two tail probabilities by normal tails.

statement: If rho_k > tau, then w_{k+1} > tau sqrt(B_k) and B_{k+1} < (1-tau^2) B_k. If no tau-regular tail appears after K, then the remaining tail mass beta_K is at most tau/(1-sqrt(1-tau^2)) sqrt(B_K) < 2 sqrt(B_K)/tau.
proof_sketch: Use C_k <= w_{k+1} B_k to force a large next weight. The variance decay telescopes after writing u_k=sqrt(B_k), giving the tail-mass bound.

statement: For any finite head and arbitrary tail of total mass beta, Pr[S >= p] is at least the explicit finite sum over head subsets of psi_beta(p-w(A)), where psi_beta(r)=1 for r<=0, (p beta-r)/(beta-r) for 0<r<=p beta, and 0 for r>p beta.
proof_sketch: The tail lies in [0,beta] and has mean p beta. The inequality E T <= r + (beta-r) Pr[T>=r] gives the lower bound for Pr[T>=r].

statement: In the strict reduced regime with support n=9, the inequality Pr[S>p]>p holds for every p in [1/6,1/5].
proof_sketch: No losing set has size at least 5. Losing pairs have matching number at most 3, losing triples have matching number at most 2, and losing 4-sets are intersecting, giving layer bounds 21, 56, 56; the resulting polynomial lower bound is positive on [1/6,1/5].

approach: Worst-case head threshold only
reason: It proves the regular-tail case when alpha_k <= sigma_k, but is too crude for dominant heads because it ignores favorable head outcomes.

approach: Absolute Berry-Esseen in extreme one-head tails
reason: The normal margin q barPhi(pt)-p barPhi(qt) decays in the far tail, while the uniform Berry-Esseen error does not; a relative moderate-deviation bound seems needed.

approach: Critical-index decay alone
reason: Geometric decay reduces the tail mass, but it still leaves a finite head that may be too large for the existing support-7/8 layer counts without an additional robust finite-head certificate.

statement: The naive universal good-merge statement is false for arbitrary reduced weights: for p=0.3 and seven equal weights 1/7, every mergeable pair satisfies Pr[p-x<R<=p] < Pr[p-x-y<R<=p-y], while the instance is not a counterexample.
proof_sketch: For any pair, the upper residual window is exactly the Bin(5,p) level K=2 and the lower window is exactly K=1, whose probability ratio is 2p/(1-p)<1 for p<1/3.

statement: For the two smallest mergeable weights a<=b, if the lower residual window has sufficiently large losing upper-shadow expansion, then the pair is a good merge.
proof_sketch: Every losing one-step extension of a lower-window set lands in the upper window because all residual weights are at least b. Counting p-biased shadow edges gives mu(U) >= (p/(qK)) sum_T |Gamma(T)| mu(T).

statement: If n=7, 0<w_i<p, sum w_i=1, and 1/4<=p<1/3, then Pr[S>p]>p.
proof_sketch: Use the graph W of winning pairs. Losing triples force W on their complements to block all K4 perfect matchings, giving strong d3 bounds in terms of |W|; losing 4-sets are 3-intersecting, so d4<=5. A four-case polynomial check proves mu_p(D)<1-p.

statement: In support 7, the previous upper-interval obstruction with three disjoint losing pairs cannot occur in a strict counterexample for 1/4<=p<1/3.
proof_sketch: The support-7 proof does not assume anything about the matching number of the losing-pair graph and therefore eliminates both the matching-number-at-most-2 case and the three-disjoint-losing-pairs case.

approach: Naive universal good merge
reason: Seven equal weights with p in (2/7,1/3) have every mergeable pair bad, so local merge inequalities alone do not contradict reducedness.

approach: Direct FKG/Harris comparison of the two residual windows
reason: The upper and lower windows are differences of downsets rather than monotone events, and the equal-weight example reverses the desired comparison.

approach: Crude support-7 layer counts

statement: Finite-support and structural lemmas for strict reduced Bernoulli weighted-sum counterexamples in the base interval.
proof_excerpt: The heart of the argument is to write Pr[S≤p] as ∑_{j=0}^n ℓ_j p^j q^{n−j}, where ℓ_j counts “losing” j‐sets, and then derive sharp upper bounds on the ℓ_j by invoking graph‐ and hypergraph‐matching lemmas (no two disjoint losing pairs, intersecting 3‐uniform families, and a degree‐sequence inequality) so that the resulting function F(p)=q−∑ℓ_j p^j q^{n−j} has positive derivative and endpoint value on each given p‐interval.  The main difficulty—controlling ℓ_2, ℓ_3 (and in the general case ℓ_k) under the strict reduced‐support hypothesis—is overcome by those combinatorial bounds, together with a minimal‐counterexample “merge” lemma that yields the final residual‐probability inequality.
problem_solved:
The proof establishes the stated partial theorem:

For \(0<p<1\), positive weights \(0<w_i<p\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\), independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), and \(S=\sum_i w_i v_i\):

1. If \(n=7\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
2. If \(n=8\) and \(p\in[1/6,1/5]\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
3. If \(n=7\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), and the losing-pair graph has matching number at most \(2\), then \(\Pr[S>p]>p\).
4. For every losing \(k\)-uniform family \(\mathcal H\), with nondecreasing degree sequence \(d_{(1)}\le\cdots\le d_{(n)}\) and \(s=\lfloor 1/p\rfloor\),
\[
p\sum_{j=1}^s d_{(j)}+(1-sp)d_{(s+1)}\le p|\mathcal H|.
\]
5. For a minimum-support strict counterexample among positive weights \(<p\), if two weights \(x\le y\) satisfy \(x+y<p\), then with \(R\) the residual random sum,
\[
\Pr[p-x<R\le p]
<
\Pr[p-x-y<R\le p-y].
\]

The proof does not establish the original problem for arbitrary nonnegative weights or all \(p\).

statement: Complement upper-tail reduction: for q=1-p, the desired inequality at p is equivalent to Pr_q[sum_i w_i xi_i > q] <= q for iid Bernoulli(q) variables xi_i.
proof_sketch: Let xi_i=1-v_i. Then sum_i w_i v_i < p is equivalent to sum_i w_i xi_i > 1-p=q. Thus Pr_p[sum w_i v_i >= p] >= p iff Pr_p[sum w_i v_i < p] <= 1-p iff Pr_q[sum w_i xi_i > q] <= q.

statement: In a strict reduced counterexample with p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the complement upper-tail family F={A: w(A)>1-p} is an increasing linear-threshold family with mu_{1-p}(F)>1-p and is k-wise intersecting.
proof_sketch: The bad family D={A:w(A)<p} has mu_p(D)>1-p. Complementation maps mu_p(D) to mu_{1-p}(F), where F={B:w(B)>1-p}. If k sets B_1,...,B_k in F had empty intersection, their complements A_j would cover all coordinates, so 1 <= sum_j w(A_j) < kp <= 1, contradiction.

proof_sketch: Round 4 combined prior support-7 lower-range counting with a new upper-range argument using winning-pair blockers for losing triples, 3-intersection of losing 4-sets, and polynomial layer checks.

Proving a biased-measure bound for k-wise intersecting LTFs in q in [(k-1)/k,k/(k+1)] would close the base interval. Keep finite support n=8 as the backup concrete target.

statement: Complement upper-tail and k-wise-intersection reduction for strict counterexamples to the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality.
Complement upper-tail and k-wise-intersection reduction for strict counterexamples to the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality.
proof_excerpt: By recasting the event {∑wi vi≥p} as the complement of the p-biased family D={A:w(A)<p} and then using the bijection A↦[m]\A to the q-biased family F={B:w(B)>q}, one shows exactly that Pr[∑wi vi≥p]≥p⇔μq(F)≤q. The key combinatorial lemma then proves that whenever rp≤1 no r sets in F can be disjoint: assigning each element to at most r–1 of the B₁,…,Bᵣ gives ∑j w(Bj)≤r–1, while each w(Bj)>q=1–p forces ∑j w(Bj)>r(1–p)≥r–1, a contradiction that establishes F is r-wise intersecting.
problem_solved:
The proof establishes the claimed partial structural reduction: for every positive integer \(m\), every nonnegative weight vector \((w_1,\dots,w_m)\) with total weight \(1\), every \(p\in(0,1)\), \(q=1-p\), independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), and independent Bernoulli\((q)\) variables \(\xi_i\), the inequality
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p
\]
is equivalent, for the same weights, to
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\xi_i>q\right]\le q.
\]
With
\[
\mathcal D=\{A\subseteq[m]:w(A)<p\},\qquad
\mathcal F=\{B\subseteq[m]:w(B)>q\},
\]
complementation maps \(\mathcal D\) bijectively onto \(\mathcal F\), and \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\mu_q(\mathcal F)\). Also, for every positive integer \(r\) with \(rp\le1\), \(\mathcal F\) is \(r\)-wise intersecting; in particular, if \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), then \(\mathcal F\) is \(k\)-wise intersecting. The proof also correctly notes that \(\mathcal F\) is an increasing linear-threshold family.

It does not classify all \(p\) for which the original inequality holds universally.

statement: In the strict reduced regime with support n=8, Pr[sum w_i v_i > p] > p for every p in [1/5,1/3).
proof_sketch: For [1/5,1/4), use matching bounds, EKR for losing triples, and Kruskal–Katona to get d2<=21, d3<=21, d4<=15. For [1/4,1/3), use winning-pair blockers, 2-intersecting complement bounds d4<=17 and d5<=6, Kneser supersaturation for losing triples, and a high-pair refinement on [1/4,2/7]; explicit polynomial checks give mu_p(D)<1-p.

statement: In support n=8, the lower-shadow/Kruskal–Katona improvement gives d4<=15 on [1/5,1/4) once d3<=21.
proof_sketch: The lower shadow of D4 is contained in D3. If d4>=16, Kruskal–Katona forces at least 23 losing triples, contradicting the intersecting bound d3<=21.

statement: For p<1/3 and n=8, complements of losing 4-sets form a 2-intersecting 4-family, so d4<=17; complements of losing 5-sets form a 2-intersecting 3-family, so d5<=6.
proof_sketch: Two high complements meeting too little would have total weight at least 2(1-p) but at most 1+p, impossible for p<1/3. Then apply the standard 2-intersection bounds.

statement: For losing triples H=D3, if m is the number of winning pairs, then the number of disjoint pairs in H is at most 10m, while Kneser supersaturation gives I(H)>=d3(d3-21)/7.
proof_sketch: Each disjoint pair of losing triples has a complementary winning pair, and each pair supports exactly 10 partitions into two triples. Hoffman supersaturation in KG(8,3) gives the lower bound.

approach: Crude independent layer bounds for n=8 above p=1/4
reason: Bounds such as d2<=28, d3<=56, d4<=35, d5<=21 are far too weak near p=1/4 and even the improved d4<=17, d5<=6 needs a tradeoff with d2 and d3.

approach: Extending support n=9 beyond [1/6,1/5]
reason: The n=9 middle range introduces losing 5-sets and much larger matching-number-2 triple families; the n=8 high-pair/blocker tradeoff did not immediately produce a clean polynomial certificate.

statement: For p <= 1/k, the k-wise intersection property of F={A: w(A)>1-p} is automatic for every threshold family of this form.
proof_sketch: If B_j are complements with w(B_j)<p, then w(union B_j)<kp<=1, so the union cannot cover the positive support.

statement: Support-excess lemma: if n=k+s and B_1,...,B_t are disjoint bad sets of sizes r_j, then sum_j (r_j-1)<s.
proof_sketch: The union has weight < tp, while its complement has too few coordinates to carry weight >1-tp under the cap w_i<p.

statement: For every k>=3 and p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the target inequality holds whenever the positive support size is at most k+2.
proof_sketch: For n=k+1 only empty and singleton bad sets occur; for n=k+2 the bad family has rank at most 2 and the bad-pair graph has matching number at most 1. Counting gives mu_p(G)<=q^k(1+kp)<=q.

statement: For k=3 and p in [1/4,1/3], the target inequality holds for every reduced instance with at most five positive weights.
proof_sketch: This is the k=3 specialization of the support k+2 theorem, with the explicit bound mu_p(G)<=q^3(1+3p)<=q.

approach: Use k-wise intersection alone
reason: For these linear thresholds the k-wise property is automatic when p<=1/k, so it adds no extra leverage beyond the original lower-tail problem.

approach: Naive moment or Cantelli bounds
reason: The threshold is exactly the mean, so one-sided variance inequalities have zero gap and do not force Pr[S>=E S]>=p.

approach: Pairwise merging of weights
reason: The local CDF comparison changes sign depending on the outside partial sum, so merging small coordinates is not monotone without additional structure.

approach: Crude layer counts for the first open k=3,n=6 case
reason: The automatic bounds e2<=10 and e3<=10 leave a small numerical gap near p=1/3; the weighted shifted structure of the bad layers is still needed.

statement: In the strict reduced regime with support n=8, Pr[sum_i w_i v_i > p] > p for every p in [1/5,1/3); combined with earlier work this eliminates support 8 throughout [1/6,1/3).
proof_sketch: For [1/5,1/4), losing sets have rank at most 4, matching and EKR bounds give d2<=21 and d3<=21, and Kruskal-Katona improves d4<=15. For [1/4,1/3), complements of high losing layers are 2-intersecting, losing triples are controlled by Kneser supersaturation in terms of winning pairs, and additional high-pair/blocker refinements close the remaining polynomial gaps.

statement: For p<=1/k, the complement family F={A: w(A)>1-p} is automatically k-wise intersecting, so k-wise intersection alone adds no information beyond the weighted lower-tail setup.
proof_sketch: If k complements B_j all have w(B_j)<p, then w(union B_j)<kp<=1, so they cannot cover the positive support; equivalently the original high-weight sets have common intersection.

statement: Support-excess lemma: if p in [1/(k+1),1/k], support size n=k+s, and B_1,...,B_t are disjoint bad sets of sizes r_j, then sum_j (r_j-1)<s.
proof_sketch: The union of the bad sets has weight <tp, so the complement has weight >1-tp. If sum_j(r_j-1)>=s, then the complement has at most k-t coordinates, each of weight <p, contradicting 1-tp>=(k-t)p.

statement: For p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the desired inequality holds in the reduced regime whenever the positive support size is at most k+2.
proof_sketch: The support-excess lemma forces rank at most 2 and bad-pair matching number at most 1. Counting gives mu_p(G)<=q^k(1+kp)<=q.

statement: Literature endpoint: Frankl-Tokushige gives mu_theta(F)<=theta for every k-wise intersecting family F when theta<=(k-1)/k; this explains the reciprocal endpoints but does not cover the needed high-bias interval theta in [(k-1)/k,k/(k+1)].
proof_sketch: This theorem reaches q=(k-1)/k, corresponding to p=1/k, but our complement formulation for p in [1/(k+1),1/k] requires q=1-p above that endpoint, where general k-wise intersecting families can be too large.

approach: Use k-wise intersection alone on the complement family.
reason: The property is automatic for threshold families with p<=1/k and is false as a sufficient measure bound above the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint for general k-wise intersecting families.

approach: Crude independent layer bounds for support 8 above p=1/4.
reason: Bounds on d2,d3,d4,d5 separately are too weak; the successful proof needs tradeoffs involving winning pairs, blockers, high-pair graphs, and Kneser supersaturation.

approach: Direct extension of the support-8 layer proof to support 9.
reason: The n=9 middle and upper ranges introduce losing 5-sets and much larger matching-number-two triple families, so the n=8 estimates do not transfer without new tradeoffs.

In the strict reduced support-9 regime, Pr[S>p]>p holds for every p in [1/5, 1/4].
proof_sketch: Use the winning-pair graph W. For p<=1/4, disjoint losing triples force complementary W-triangles, giving a Kneser supersaturation bound on d3; losing 4-sets and 5-sets force W-blockers in K5 and K4, giving d4<=floor(35m/6) and d5<=min(21,7m). A 37-case exact Sturm check over m=0,...,36 proves the resulting layer polynomial is below (1+r)^8 on r in [1/4,1/3].

statement: For support 9, the normalized deficiency cascade d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}<=(9-k)E_k holds for k>=2, with E_2<=28+m-8/r.
proof_sketch: For each losing (k+1)-set B, sum the deficiencies of its k-subsets: the sum equals p+k(p-w(B)). Then count how many losing supersets each losing k-set can have. The initial pair-deficiency bound follows from the total pair-weight identity and the fact that each winning pair contributes more than -p.

statement: For p<1/3 in support 9, d5<=min(21,7m) and d6<=min(7,T_m), where m is the number of winning pairs and T_m is the maximum number of triangles in an m-edge graph.
proof_sketch: A losing 5-set complement must block all perfect matchings of K4, giving at least three winning edges and hence d5<=7m; complement 2-intersection gives d5<=21. A losing 6-set complement must be a W-triangle, giving d6<=T_m, while complement 2-intersection gives d6<=7.

statement: A support-9 instance with tail weight b=w9 is equivalent to an 8-head two-threshold inequality: Pr[S>p]=1-q mu_p(E0)-p mu_p(E1), where E0={x(A)<=p} and E1={x(A)<=p-b}.
proof_sketch: Condition on the ninth Bernoulli variable. If the head sum exceeds p, success is automatic; if it lies in (p-b,p], success requires the tail variable to be 1; otherwise it fails.

approach: Use the p<=1/4 winning-pair blocker for D4 above p=1/4
reason: The key contradiction becomes w(A)+w(e1)+w(e2)+w_z<4p, which no longer contradicts total mass 1 when p>1/4.

approach: Deficiency cascade plus crude d4<=56 on [1/4,1/3)
reason: The relaxed bounds allow a non-feasible-looking obstruction at r=2/5, m=3 with layer profile (1,9,33,56,56,6,1), whose polynomial exceeds (1+r)^8 by 80174/390625.

approach: Direct reduction of the 8-coordinate head to the known support-8 theorem
reason: After removing a tail of weight b, the head weights have total mass 1-b and the relevant thresholds are p and p-b while the Bernoulli parameter remains p, so the one-threshold normalized support-8 result does not apply directly.

statement: For every k>=3 and p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the desired complement high-bias LTF bound mu_q(F_q)<=q holds whenever the positive support size is at most k+3.
proof_sketch: After reducing to weights 0<w_i<p, a support-excess lemma gives rank and matching restrictions on the bad family D_p={w<p}. In the new n=k+3 case, bad triples form a 2-intersecting 3-graph and bad pairs satisfy a matching bound, giving e2+(p/q)e3 <= 2k+3+(k+1)p/q; the layer count then collapses to q^k(1+kp)<=q.

statement: In the k=3 high-bias interval q in [2/3,3/4], every strict LTF counterexample must have at least 7 positive weights.
proof_sketch: This is the k=3 specialization of the support-size <=k+3 theorem, improving the previous support-size <=5 result by handling the n=6 rank-three case.

statement: A precise thinning/sprinkling reduction was derived: starting from t0=(k-1)/k, it is enough to bound the sprinkling error from mu_t0 to mu_q by q-t0.
proof_sketch: Generate A~mu_q by sampling B~mu_t0 and adding each missing coordinate with probability (q-t0)/(1-t0); the endpoint theorem bounds the part w(B)>t0 by t0, leaving only the boundary sprinkling term.

statement: The empty-intersection spectral form was expanded in the q-biased Fourier basis, isolating the extra first-level constraint supplied by the LTF condition.
proof_sketch: For the k-fold kernel K=prod_i(1-prod_j X_i^j), each coordinate has an explicit expansion in biased characters; the LTF condition gives positive weighted first Fourier correlation with w(X)-q.

approach: Endpoint-to-interior differential bound via Russo influence
reason: A universal pointwise influence bound I_t(F)<=1 is false; the admissible four-equal-weights LTF F={|A|>=3} has I_{2/3}=16/9.

approach: Pointwise random thinning lower bound
reason: For k=3, q=0.70, thinning three weights 0.24,0.24,0.24 from total 0.72 requires all three to survive, giving probability alpha^3<alpha.

approach: Direct Hoffman spectral extension above theta=(k-1)/k
reason: The LTF property controls a weighted first Fourier coefficient, but the empty-intersection form above the endpoint contains uncontrolled higher-order mixed Fourier terms.

statement: For every k≥3 and p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the desired complement high-bias LTF bound, equivalently the original Bernoulli inequality, holds whenever the positive support size is at most k+3.
proof_sketch: Reduce to positive weights all less than p. If n=k+s, disjoint bad sets B_j of sizes r_j satisfy sum_j(r_j−1)<s. For n=k+3 this forces rank at most 3, no three disjoint bad pairs, and no bad pair disjoint from a bad triple. Bad triples are then 2-intersecting, and bad pairs are controlled by a matching bound, giving e2+(p/q)e3≤2k+3+(k+1)p/q. The layer count simplifies to q^k(1+kp)≤q.

statement: In the strict reduced support-9 regime, Pr[S>p]>p holds for every p in [1/5,1/4].
proof_sketch: Let W be the graph of winning pairs. For p≤1/4, disjoint losing triples force complementary W-triangles, giving a Kneser supersaturation bound for d3. Losing 4-sets and 5-sets force W-blockers in K5 and K4, giving d4≤floor(35m/6) and d5≤min(21,7m). A rational Sturm check over m=0,...,36 proves the resulting layer polynomial is below (1+r)^8 for r=p/(1-p) in [1/4,1/3].

statement: For support 9, the deficiency cascade d_{k+1}+kE_{k+1}≤(9−k)E_k holds for k≥2, with E_2≤28+m−8/r.
proof_sketch: For each losing (k+1)-set, sum the normalized deficiencies of its k-subsets. Counting how many losing supersets a losing k-set can have gives the cascade; the initial E_2 bound comes from the total pair-weight identity and the fact that each winning pair contributes more than −p.

statement: A support-9 instance with tail weight b is equivalent to an 8-head two-threshold inequality q μ_p(E0)+p μ_p(E1)<q, where E0={head sum≤p} and E1={head sum≤p−b}.
proof_sketch: Condition on the ninth Bernoulli variable. The head already exceeding p succeeds automatically, the head in (p−b,p] succeeds only if the tail variable is 1, and the head at most p−b fails.

approach: Extend the p≤1/4 winning-pair blocker for D4 directly to p>1/4.
reason: The contradiction becomes a bound by 4p, which no longer contradicts total mass 1 once p>1/4.

approach: Use the support-9 deficiency cascade together with only crude d4≤56 in the upper interval.
reason: The relaxed constraints allow a non-feasible-looking obstruction near r=2/5 with layer profile (1,9,33,56,56,6,1).

approach: Endpoint-to-interior differential proof via a pointwise influence bound I_t(F)≤1.
reason: The admissible LTF F={|A|≥3} on four equal weights has I_{2/3}=16/9.

approach: Pointwise thinning from q to the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint.
reason: Three weights 0.24,0.24,0.24 at q=0.70 require all three thinned elements to survive, giving probability α^3<α.

approach: Direct Hoffman extension above the Frankl-Tokushige endpoint using only k-wise intersection.
reason: The LTF condition gives useful first-level Fourier information, but the empty-intersection form contains uncontrolled higher-level mixed terms.

statement: For every integer k≥3 and p in [1/(k+1),1/k], the Bernoulli weighted-sum inequality holds whenever the weight vector has at most k+3 positive coordinates.
For k=3, p in [1/4,1/3], the desired inequality mu_p({B: w(B)<p}) <= 1-p holds whenever the positive support has size at most 7.
proof_sketch: Pad to seven coordinates, scale by p, and count bad layers up to size 4. Bad triples and bad 4-sets are controlled by the good-pair graph; a fractional perfect matching argument bounds the number of bad pairs for p <= 2/7, and the remaining p >= 2/7 cases are handled by a finite graph split according to the number of good pairs.

statement: In the seven-coordinate normalized problem, every bad triple has complement spanning at least three good pairs, and every bad 4-set has complement equal to a good triangle.
proof_sketch: The complement of a bad triple has weight > 2; two disjoint bad pairs inside it would have total weight < 2, impossible. The complement of a bad 4-set is a 3-set of weight > 2, so every pair inside it must be good.

statement: For seven coordinates, if p <= 2/7 then the bad-pair graph has at most 15 edges.
proof_sketch: If it had at least 16 edges, its complement would have at most 5 edges, forcing a fractional perfect matching in the bad-pair graph. Weighting the bad-pair inequalities by this matching gives total scaled weight < 7/2, contradicting 1/p >= 7/2.

statement: Any strict k=3 high-bias LTF counterexample must have at least 8 positive weights.
proof_sketch: If some weight is at least p the theorem is trivial; otherwise the support-at-most-7 theorem rules out all smaller strict counterexamples.

problem_solved:
For every integer \(k\ge3\), every \(p\in[1/(k+1),1/k]\), every finite set of nonnegative weights summing to \(1\) with at most \(k+3\) positive entries, and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), the proof establishes
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

statement: In the strict reduced support-9 regime, for every p in [1/5,1/4], positive weights 0p]>p for iid Bernoulli(p) variables.
The proposed crude 9-head deterministic tail bound Phi>=p is false under the stated constraints.
proof_sketch: Take p=1/3 and eighteen equal weights 1/18. For the first nine weights as head, beta=1/2 and the crude tail-filling value is Phi=1915/19683<1/3, even though the true probability is larger than 1/3.

statement: A smoothed finite-head/tail certificate holds: for a head of size h<=9, an arbitrary buffer of mass gamma, and a regular remainder with variance sigma^2 and Berry-Esseen parameter rho, Pr[S>=p] is at least the head average of chi_{gamma,sigma}(p(alpha+gamma)-a(A)) minus C_BE K(p) rho.
proof_sketch: Condition on the head and buffer. Apply Berry-Esseen to the regular remainder, then minimize the resulting Gaussian tail function over all buffer distributions with range [0,gamma] and mean p gamma using the lower convex envelope.

statement: Critical-index decay after the first nine weights reduces the nonregular part of the tail to an explicit bounded buffer: if no tau-regular tail occurs before index ell, then gamma=sum_{i=10}^ell w_i <= c_tau(sqrt(B_9)-sqrt(B_ell)), and in particular gamma <= c_tau(a_9/tau - sqrt(B_ell)).
proof_sketch: Nonregularity gives w_{k+1}>tau sqrt(B_k) and sqrt(B_{k+1})<=sqrt(1-tau^2)sqrt(B_k). Summing the resulting telescoping bound on the removed weights gives the stated buffer-mass estimate.

approach: Use only the elementary range/mean tail function psi_beta after the first nine weights
reason: The equal-18 example gives Phi=1915/19683<1/3, so the crude tail compression loses too much information about regular tails.

approach: Induct using only the original inequality for the tail at its mean
reason: That gives only Pr[T>=p beta]>=p, but head outcomes often require quantitative lower bounds for thresholds below the tail mean.

approach: Fixed 9-head absolute Berry-Esseen alone
reason: In far-tail or only moderately regular cases the Gaussian margin can be smaller than the uniform Berry-Esseen error; a smoothed buffer certificate or relative tail estimate is needed.

statement: Berry–Esseen effective-dimension reduction: for p in [1/4,1/3], if rho=(sum w_i^3)/(sum w_i^2)^{3/2} <= 5 sqrt(2)/28, then Pr[sum w_i v_i >= p] >= p. Hence any counterexample has (sum w_i^2)^3/(sum w_i^3)^2 < 15.68.
proof_sketch: Apply the uniform Berry–Esseen inequality to S-p. The BE error at the mean is at most 0.56*(p^2+q^2)/sqrt(pq)*rho; if this is <=1/2-p, the normal 1/2 mass at the mean gives the desired lower bound.

statement: Exact pair-merging formula: replacing two weights a>=b with a+b changes the bad probability by pq(Pr[R in [p-a-b,p-a)) - Pr[R in [p-b,p))).
proof_sketch: Condition on the outside sum R and compare the two-point split variable a xi+b eta with the merged variable (a+b) zeta. The conditional difference is +pq on one slab, -pq on the adjacent upper slab, and zero elsewhere.

statement: Assuming the known support <=9 theorem, the full k=3 high-bias theorem follows from the adjacent-slab monotonicity condition for the two smallest weights.
proof_sketch: If the lower slab never has more mass than the upper slab, merging the two smallest weights cannot decrease the bad probability. Repeated merging would turn any support >=10 counterexample into a support-9 counterexample, contradicting the finite-support result.

statement: A pointwise cyclic/orbit interpolation of the p=1/k proof fails throughout p in (1/4,1/3).
proof_sketch: For four equal weights and four equally spaced arcs of length p on the circle, the superlevel set has measure 4p-1<p. Thus individual diagonal orbits can be bad even though the independent product measure is not.

approach: Pointwise cyclic/Liggett interpolation
reason: Four equal weights with four equally spaced arcs of length p give orbit-good fraction 4p-1

approach: Berry–Esseen alone
reason: It only forces L3/L2 effective dimension below about 16 for counterexamples, not actual support <=9; long tiny tails can coexist with small effective dimension.

approach: Unconditional pairwise merging
reason: The exact formula has two slabs with opposite signs, so merging is monotone exactly when the adjacent-slab inequality holds.

statement: A crude 9-head deterministic range/mean tail replacement is false.
proof_sketch: At p=1/3 with eighteen equal weights 1/18 and the first nine weights as head, the crude tail-filling functional equals 1915/19683, far below p, although the true probability is above p.

statement: A smoothed finite-head/tail certificate holds with an arbitrary buffer and a Berry-Esseen-regular remainder.
proof_sketch: Condition on the head and buffer. Berry-Esseen lower-bounds the regular remainder tail by a Gaussian tail minus C_BE K(p) rho. Minimizing over all buffer distributions with range [0,gamma] and mean p gamma gives the lower convex envelope chi_{gamma,sigma}, yielding the stated head average certificate.

statement: Critical-index decay bounds the nonregular buffer after the first nine weights.
proof_sketch: If the tail after k is not tau-regular then the next weight is larger than tau sqrt(B_k) and sqrt(B_{k+1}) contracts by at least sqrt(1-tau^2). Summing this geometric decay gives gamma <= c_tau(sqrt(B_9)-sqrt(B_ell)), and hence gamma <= c_tau(a_9/tau - sqrt(B_ell)) when starting after the ninth weight.

statement: For p in [1/4,1/3], Berry-Esseen rules out counterexamples with rho=(sum w_i^3)/(sum w_i^2)^(3/2) <= 5sqrt2/28.
proof_sketch: Apply the uniform Berry-Esseen inequality to S-p. The normal mass at the mean is 1/2, and the error is at most 0.56 (p^2+q^2)/sqrt(pq) rho. At p=1/3 this yields the threshold 5sqrt2/28, the minimum over the interval.

statement: Exact pair-merging formula: replacing weights a>=b with a+b changes the bad probability by pq times the difference between a lower residual slab and an upper residual slab.
proof_sketch: Condition on the residual R. The split and merged two-coordinate systems differ only when p-R lies in (a,a+b] or in (0,b], giving the formula mu_split - mu_merge = pq(Pr[R in [p-a-b,p-a)) - Pr[R in [p-b,p))).

statement: Universal adjacent-slab monotonicity for the two smallest weights is false even in strict reduced support 10.
proof_sketch: Take p=3/10, weights w_1=...=w_8=41/400 and w_9=w_10=9/100. For the two smallest weights a=b=9/100, the residual is R=(41/400)K with K binomial(8,0.3). The lower slab [p-a-b,p-a)=[0.12,0.21) contains the atom K=2, while the upper slab [p-b,p)=[0.21,0.3) contains no atom.

approach: Extend the reciprocal orbit proof pointwise to p in (1/4,1/3).
reason: Four equal weights on four equally spaced circle arcs have orbit-good measure 4p-1, which is less than p for p<1/3.
