statement: In the strict reduced support-9 regime, for every p in [1/5,1/4], positive weights 0p]>p for iid Bernoulli(p) variables.
proof_excerpt: The proof begins by reparameterizing with r=p/(1−p) so that Pr[∑w_iv_i≤p]=q^9∑_{j=0}^9d_jr^j, and reduces to showing ∑d_jr^j<(1+r)^8 on r∈[1/4,1/3].  The heart of the argument is sharp extremal bounds on the “losing” set‐counts d_j: for j=2 via the number m of heavy pairs, for j=3 by relating disjoint triples in D_3 to triangles in the Kneser graph and applying its least‐eigenvalue bound, for j=4 by the classical bound 56 on intersecting 4-families via cyclic‐interval counting, and for j=5 by a 2-intersecting–family bound obtained from a positive semidefinite combination of Johnson‐scheme matrices.  Substituting these d_j≤H_m,A_m,B_m into the polynomial and checking monotonicity in r (together with a finite case check at r=1/4) yields the strict inequality and hence Pr[∑w_iv_i>p]>p.
problem_solved:
The proof establishes the restricted support-9 statement: for every \(p\in[1/5,1/4]\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with \(\sum_i w_i=1\) and \(w_i<p\) for all \(i\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
The combinatorial bounds on \(d_3,d_4,d_5\), the spectral bounds used for \(KG(9,3)\) and \(J(9,4)\), and the final finite polynomial check are all valid for the stated parameter range.

statement: Strict reduced support-9 theorem on the base interval: for every p in [1/6,1/3), every nine positive weights 0p]>p for iid Bernoulli(p) variables.
With C_BE=0.56 and tau=1/32, if a split after the first 9 weights has regular remainder rho<=1/32 and alpha+gamma<=sigma, then Pr[S>=p]>p for every p in [1/4,1/3].
proof_sketch: For every head and buffer outcome, the centered tail threshold is at most p(alpha+gamma)<=sigma/3. Berry-Esseen gives probability at least barPhi(1/3)-7/(160 sqrt(3)), which is larger than 1/3>=p.

statement: The effective-dimension case is closed: if rho_0=(sum w_i^3)/(sum w_i^2)^{3/2} <= eta(p), then Pr[S>=p]>=p; uniformly on [1/4,1/3] it suffices that rho_0<=5 sqrt(2)/28.
proof_sketch: Apply Berry-Esseen to S-p at the mean. The lower bound 1/2-C_BE K(p) rho_0 is at least p exactly under the displayed eta(p) condition.

statement: For sigma>0, chi_{gamma,sigma}(r) has an explicit one-dimensional lower-convex-envelope formula: it is f(p gamma) in the convex case r>=gamma, the endpoint chord in the concave case r<=0, and otherwise is determined by a unique tangency point s solving f'(s)=(f(gamma)-f(s))/(gamma-s).
proof_sketch: For f(u)=barPhi((r-u)/sigma), the sign of f'' is the sign of r-u, so f is convex then concave. The lower convex envelope is therefore either f itself, the endpoint chord, or f followed by a tangent-to-endpoint chord.

statement: The sigma=0 critical-index range/mean certificate is false even after excluding the effective-dimension case: for p=1/3 and fourteen equal weights, the first-9-head psi certificate equals 49159/255879<1/3 while rho_0=1/sqrt(14)>5 sqrt(2)/28.
proof_sketch: With first 9 equal weights as head and buffer mass 5/14, K~Bin(9,1/3) gives psi values 0 for K<=3, 3/13 for K=4, and 1 for K>=5. The resulting exact value is about 0.1921, below p.

statement: The gamma=0 regular-tail absolute Berry-Esseen certificate is not uniformly valid: for p=1/3, one head weight 21/64, eight head weights 1/1536, and 1024 tail weights 1/1536, the Gaussian head average is less than p+10^{-26}, while the BE penalty is about 0.0206.
proof_sketch: The regular tail has rho=1/32. If the dominant head weight is absent, the Gaussian threshold is at least 10.78 standard deviations; if it is present, the contribution is at most 1. Thus the Gaussian surplus over p is exponentially tiny, much smaller than the O(rho) BE error.

approach: Use only the sigma=0 range/mean buffer function psi_gamma under the critical-index mass bound
reason: Fourteen equal weights at p=1/3 satisfy the effective-dimension exclusion but give a psi certificate value about 0.1921<1/3.

approach: Use absolute Berry-Esseen for every gamma=0 regular tail
reason: In the one-dominant-head far-tail regime, the Gaussian margin above p is exponentially small while the Berry-Esseen error is only polynomial in the tail regularity parameter.

approach: Treat a_9 small as automatically easy
reason: The regular-tail counterexample has a_9=1/1536 and still defeats the absolute-BE certificate.

statement: In support n>=10, all pairs among the bottom 4 weights are mergeable, and among the bottom 5 weights all pairs except possibly (w4,w5) are mergeable.
proof_sketch: Since 1>2w3+6w5 and w3<=w5, one gets w3+w5<1/4<=p; this dominates every bottom-4 pair and every bottom-5 pair except possibly (w4,w5).

statement: In a minimal-support counterexample with gap epsilon=Pr(D)-q>0, every mergeable pair satisfies L-U>=epsilon/(pq), where L and U are the lower and upper adjacent slabs in the merge formula.
proof_sketch: For a mergeable pair, the merged instance has bad probability at most q by minimality, while the original has bad probability q+epsilon; the exact merge formula gives mu-mu_merge=pq(L-U).

statement: For a mergeable pair (i,j), pq(L-U)=Cov(1_{A_i},v_j)=Cov(1_{A_j},v_i)=the p-biased level-2 Fourier coefficient of 1_{S>=p} on {i,j}.
proof_sketch: Conditioning on the other variables, the second discrete derivative of the threshold indicator is +1 on the lower slab and -1 on the upper slab; the pivotal covariance gives the same difference.

statement: If all bottom-4 mergeable pairs are bad, then 6 epsilon^2 <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon); if all bottom-5 mergeable pairs are bad, then 9 epsilon^2 <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon), hence epsilon <= 2/15 throughout p in [1/4,1/3] in the bottom-5 case.
proof_sketch: Minimality makes each relevant bottom-pair level-2 Fourier coefficient at least epsilon; Parseval bounds the sum of their squares by Var(1_{S>=p})=(p-epsilon)(q+epsilon).

statement: If all bottom-5 mergeable pairs are bad, then every bottom-5 coordinate has influence at least epsilon/q and the weighted influence sum satisfies sum_i w_i I_i <= 1-epsilon/p.
proof_sketch: The covariance identity gives epsilon <= Cov(1_{A_i},v_j) <= q I_i for every coordinate with a bad partner; the weighted influence identity E[(S-p)1_G]=pq sum_i w_i I_i and S-p<=q on G give the upper bound.

statement: There is a support-10 parametric local obstruction with all bottom-5 pairs bad but Pr(D)<=q: for p in (0.29,0.31], take five weights 0.09 and five weights 0.11.
proof_sketch: For any bottom pair, the lower slab consists of residual types (K,L)=(0,2),(1,1), while the upper slab consists of (0,3),(1,2),(2,0), giving L-U=8p^2q^5(1-3p)>0. Direct binomial enumeration gives Pr(D)<0.606<q on this interval.

approach: Universal bottom-5 good-pair principle
reason: The five-0.09/five-0.11 support-10 family has every bottom-5 pair bad, so a local existence statement is false without using Pr(D)>q and minimality.

approach: Parseval/Fourier mass alone
reason: It forces an all-bottom-5-bad minimal counterexample to have small gap epsilon, at most 2/15, but does not force epsilon=0.

approach: Mean-only slice certificate over the outside sum
reason: All-bad bottom slices can be compensated by high outside-sum slices; controlling only E[R] is too weak, and one needs product-structure or additional covariance information.

statement: In support n≥10, all pairs among the four smallest weights are mergeable for p≥1/4, and among the five smallest weights all pairs except possibly the two largest of those five are mergeable.
proof_sketch: With weights sorted increasingly, 1>2w3+6w5 and w3≤w5 imply w3+w5<1/4≤p, which dominates all bottom-four pairs and all bottom-five pairs except possibly (w4,w5).

statement: In a minimum-support strict counterexample with bad-measure gap epsilon, every mergeable pair has adjacent-slab gap at least epsilon/(pq).
proof_sketch: Merging a mergeable pair lowers support, so by minimality the merged bad probability is at most q. The exact merge formula gives original minus merged bad probability equal to pq times the lower-minus-upper slab difference.

statement: For a mergeable pair, the slab gap equals a pivotal covariance and the p-biased level-2 Fourier coefficient of the threshold event on that pair.
proof_sketch: Condition on all other coordinates. The second discrete derivative of the threshold indicator is +1 on the lower slab and -1 on the upper slab; multiplying by pq gives both the covariance and the normalized level-2 coefficient.

statement: If all bottom-five mergeable pairs are bad in a minimal counterexample, then at least nine level-2 Fourier coefficients have magnitude at least epsilon, giving 9 epsilon^2 ≤ (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon).
proof_sketch: The quantitative slab gap gives each coefficient at least epsilon; Parseval bounds the sum of all nonconstant Fourier squares by the variance of the success indicator.

statement: A Berry-Esseen effective-dimension criterion holds on p in [1/4,1/3]: if rho0=(sum w_i^3)/(sum w_i^2)^(3/2) ≤ 5 sqrt(2)/28, then the desired inequality holds.
proof_sketch: Berry-Esseen at the mean gives P[S≥p] ≥ 1/2 - 0.56 K(p) rho0, and the right side is at least p under the stated uniform bound.

statement: The proposed first-nine-head finite certificate is not universally valid using only buffer range/mean or absolute Berry-Esseen regular-tail error.
proof_sketch: Fourteen equal weights at p=1/3 defeat the range/mean buffer certificate, and a one-dominant-head plus tiny regular tail example makes the Gaussian surplus exponentially smaller than the absolute Berry-Esseen penalty.

approach: Universal bottom-five good-pair principle.
reason: A support-10 family with five weights 0.09 and five weights 0.11 has all bottom-five pairs locally bad for p around 0.3, although it is not a counterexample.

approach: Range-and-mean compression of the nonregular critical-index buffer.
reason: For fourteen equal weights at p=1/3 the resulting certificate is about 0.192, far below 1/3.

approach: Absolute Berry-Esseen for every regular tail after a finite head.
reason: In one-dominant-head far-tail cases the normal margin is exponentially tiny while the Berry-Esseen error is only polynomial in the regularity parameter.

value: Prove that the quantitative all-small-pairs-bad Fourier configuration cannot occur in a minimal counterexample with bad probability greater than q.
Develop a relative, not absolute, tail comparison for weighted Bernoulli sums around asymmetric deviations from the mean.
Convert the verified finite-support theorems up to support 9 into a robust arbitrary-support reduction.
Either prove an averaged good-merge theorem for minimum-support counterexamples with n≥10, or prove the one-coordinate asymmetric tail-ratio inequality q P[T≥p] ≥ p P[T<p-a] for 0<a<p≤1/3.

note: Do not spend final effort on universal local merge monotonicity or absolute Berry-Esseen; both are false. The most promising final targets are a minimal-counterexample averaged good-merge theorem using the quantitative Fourier constraints, and a one-coordinate asymmetric tail-ratio inequality q P[T≥p] ≥ p P[T<p-a], which if true would essentially solve p≤1/3 by conditioning on a coordinate.

statement: For the non-strict convention, the proposed lemma q P[T>=p] >= p P[T<p-a] is exactly equivalent to the original inequality after conditioning on a coordinate of weight a<p.
proof_sketch: Write S=a v+T. Then P[S>=p]-p=q P[T>=p]-p P[T<p-a]. If some weight is >=p, the original inequality is trivial.

statement: The lemma holds for every reciprocal p=1/k.
proof_sketch: Color each tail coordinate uniformly with one of k colors. For each coloring, if U is the number of color classes of mass at least 1/k and L the number below 1/k-a, then pointwise L <= (k-1)U; averaging gives the lemma.

statement: The lemma holds whenever the tail contains some coordinate x_j>=p.
proof_sketch: Then P[T>=p]>=p, while T<p-a forces xi_j=0, so P[T<p-a]<=q.

statement: For 0<p<=1/3, the lemma holds when the tail support has size at most three.
proof_sketch: For support <=2 a tail atom must be >=p. For support 3 with all atoms <p, every pair has sum >=p and no singleton is below p-a, so U=3p^2q+p^3 and L=q^3; the resulting polynomial is positive in the only possible range p>1/4.

statement: A regular-tail sufficient condition is C_BE K(p) rho <= q barPhi(pa/sigma)-p barPhi(qa/sigma).
proof_sketch: Apply Berry-Esseen to X=T-p(1-a), lower-bound P[X>=pa], upper-bound P[X<-qa], and subtract.

statement: The lemma is false for every p in (1/3,1/2).
proof_sketch: Choose 1-2p=p]=p^2 and P[T=q.

approach: Single-flip injection from {T<p-a} to {T>=p}
reason: A lower-tail outcome may require flipping several small zero-coordinates to cross p, losing more than the allowed p/q measure factor.

approach: Monotonicity under splitting tail weights
reason: Splitting can decrease the functional qP[T>=p]-pP[T<p-a]; for example at p=0.3,a=0.1, splitting a 0.4 tail atom into 0.2+0.2 lowers the margin, though it stays positive.

approach: Absolute Berry-Esseen alone in far tails
reason: The Gaussian relative margin q barPhi(pa/sigma)-p barPhi(qa/sigma) can be exponentially small when a/sigma is large, while the Berry-Esseen error is only polynomial in regularity.

approach: Pointwise interval/orbit proof for non-reciprocal p
reason: For p<1/3, disjoint active intervals can have no upper-level points but a positive lower-level gap; the reciprocal coloring argument does not extend pointwise without an additional counting idea.

statement: In a minimum-support strict reduced counterexample, every pair of coordinates, not only mergeable bottom pairs, satisfies the merge gap inequality pq(L-U)>=epsilon, equivalently the p-biased level-2 Fourier coefficient of 1_G on that pair is at least epsilon.
proof_sketch:
After merging any pair, either the new weight is

=p and the merged instance trivially has success probability at least p. The exact pair-merge formula then gives the coefficient lower bound.

statement: For every coordinate i, epsilon(1-w_i)<=p w_i I_i, where I_i is the pivotal probability; consequently w_i>=epsilon/p and epsilon<=p/n.
proof_sketch: Sum the positive pair covariances against the outside weights and use Cov(1_{A_i},R_i)<=p w_i I_i. Also A_i with v_i=1 lies inside G, so pI_i<=p-epsilon.

statement: Any such counterexample satisfies epsilon^2[(q/p) sum_i ((1-w_i)/w_i)^2 + binom(n,2)] <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon), hence epsilon < 1/(3(n-1)sqrt(n)) for p in [1/4,1/3].
proof_sketch: Use the influence lower bounds to lower-bound first-level Fourier mass and use the all-pair coefficient lower bound to lower-bound second-level Fourier mass; Parseval bounds their sum by Var(1_G). Jensen minimizes the reciprocal-weight term at equal weights.

statement: The pivotal family of the smallest coordinate is an antichain, giving epsilon <= p M_{n-1}(p)/(n-1), where M_N(p) is the largest binomial atom. In particular, for n=10 and p in [1/4,1/3], epsilon<0.0102.
proof_sketch: The pivotal interval for w_1 has length w_1 and all other weights are at least w_1, so two comparable sets cannot both be pivotal. LYM bounds its p-biased measure by the largest binomial layer, which combines with epsilon(1-w_1)<=p w_1 I_1.

approach: Bottom-pair Parseval only
reason: Using only the six bottom-four or nine bottom-five coefficients gives epsilon bounds around 0.1, far too weak.

approach: Weighted influence identity alone
reason: It yields epsilon<=p/n after the all-pair upgrade, but does not force epsilon=0.

approach: Smallest-coordinate antichain alone
reason: It gives a sharp O(n^{-3/2}) gap reduction but still permits arbitrarily small positive epsilon.

statement: Strict reduced support-9 theorem on the base interval: for every p in [1/6,1/3), every nine positive weights 0p]>p for iid Bernoulli(p) variables.
proof_excerpt:
They rewrite the tail probability as  
 Pr[∑w_i v_i≤p] = q⁹ ∑_{j=0}⁹ d_j rʲ  
and reduce the theorem to proving ∑d_j rʲ < (1+r)⁸ (since (1+r)⁸=q^−1).  
The main obstacle—bounding the counts d_j of “losing” subsets under the constraint w(A)≤p—is overcome by translating it into forbidden large matchings or necessarily intersecting families and then applying the Erdős–Gallai matching theorem to control losing pairs, together with Kruskal–Katona extremal bounds on triangles in the complementary “winning” graph, with a two‐range analysis in r (equivalently p) yielding the strict coefficient estimates needed.
problem_solved:
The proof establishes the restricted support-\(9\) strict theorem: for every \(p\in[1/6,1/4]\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with \(w_i<p\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]

statement: Berry-Esseen effective-dimension reduction, exact pair-merging formula, and a conditional finite-support reduction via good merges for the weighted Bernoulli threshold problem.
Strict reduced support-8 theorem on the base interval: for every p in [1/6,1/3), every eight positive weights 0p]>p for iid Bernoulli(p) variables.
proof_excerpt:
The proof rewrites  
 Pr[∑₁⁸wᵢvᵢ≤p] = q⁸∑_{j=0}⁸ dⱼrʲ  
and shows ∑dⱼrʲ < (1+r)⁷ by partitioning p∈[1/6,1/3) into subranges and using classical extremal set theorems (Erdős–Ko–Rado for intersecting triples, Erdős–Gallai for matchings, Kruskal–Katona for shadows, Ahlswede–Khachatrian for t-intersecting families) to tightly bound the numbers dⱼ of “losing” j-sets. The main difficulty—controlling d₃ in the middle range—is resolved by relating the number of disjoint pairs in D₃ to the count of “winning” pairs and then applying Hoffman's eigenvalue bound on the Kneser graph KG(8,3) to cap d₃, which together with the other bounds yields the desired inequality uniformly and hence Pr[∑wᵢvᵢ>p]>p.
problem_solved:
After the routine patches above, the proof establishes the restricted theorem: for every \(p\in[1/6,1/3)\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_8\) with \(w_i<p\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\), one has
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^8 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
It does not establish the original problem for arbitrary \(m\), merely nonnegative weights, weights with some \(w_i\ge p\), or a full characterization of all \(p\).

statement: Strict reduced support-9 theorem in the upper interval: for p in [1/4,1/3), positive weights 0p]>p.
Berry-Esseen effective-dimension reduction, exact pair-merging formula, and a conditional finite-support reduction via good merges for the weighted Bernoulli threshold problem.
proof_excerpt: The proof combines three key ideas. First, a refined Berry–Esseen bound shows that when the “effective dimension” parameter ρ=A₃/A₂³ᐟ² is small (so the weights are sufficiently spread out), the weighted Bernoulli sum concentrates tightly enough around its mean to satisfy Pr[S≥p]≥p. Second, an exact pair-merging formula computes the change in the failure probability µ=Pr[S
problem_solved:
The proof establishes the three stated partial/conditional assertions:

1. For \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), nonnegative weights summing to \(1\), and
\[
\rho=\frac{\sum_i w_i^3}{(\sum_i w_i^2)^{3/2}}\le \frac{5\sqrt2}{28},
\]
one has \(\Pr[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p]\ge p\).

2. For a split pair \(a\ge b>0\), \(a+b<p\), with residual sum \(R\), the identity
\[
\mu_{\rm split}-\mu_{\rm merge}
=
pq\left(\Pr[R\in[p-a-b,p-a))-\Pr[R\in[p-b,p))\right)
\]
holds exactly, with the displayed half-open endpoints correct for the strict inequality defining \(\mu\).

3. For fixed \(p\in(0,1)\) and \(N\ge1\), if there are no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexamples of support at most \(N\), and every larger strict reduced counterexample has a good-merge pair satisfying the displayed interval-probability inequality, then no strict reduced \(p\)-counterexample exists.

The proof does not characterize all \(p\) for the original problem.

statement: Quantitative pair-merge and Fourier constraints for minimum-support strict counterexamples.
Quantitative pair-merge and Fourier constraints for minimum-support strict counterexamples.
proof_excerpt: By merging any two of the smallest weights one obtains an exact “slab‐gap’’ formula μ−μ^{(ij)}=pq(L_{ij}−U_{ij}), and minimal‐support forces each bad merge to satisfy L_{ij}−U_{ij}≥ε/(pq).  Recognizing L_{ij}−U_{ij} as the level-2 p-biased Fourier coefficient (or pivotal covariance) of the indicator of {S≥p}, Parseval’s identity then shows that having all six (or nine) bottom-four (resp. bottom-five) pairs bad would exceed the total variance (p−ε)(q+ε), yielding the stated quadratic bounds on ε.
problem_solved:
The proof establishes the stated conditional structural theorem: for \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\), \(q=1-p\), \(n\ge10\), positive sorted weights \(0<w_1\le\cdots\le w_n<p\) with total mass \(1\), and \(\Pr(D)=q+\varepsilon\), assuming every mergeable pair reduction has merged bad probability at most \(q\), the bottom-four pairs and all bottom-five pairs except possibly \((w_4,w_5)\) are mergeable; each mergeable pair satisfies
\[
\mu-\mu^{(ij)}=pq(L_{ij}-U_{ij}),\qquad L_{ij}-U_{ij}\ge \varepsilon/(pq);
\]
the slab gap equals the normalized pivotal covariance and normalized level-2 \(p\)-biased Fourier coefficient;
\[
6\varepsilon^2\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon)
\]
under all-bottom-four badness, and
\[
9\varepsilon^2\le (p-\varepsilon)(q+\varepsilon)
\]
under all-bottom-five mergeable-pair badness; and in the latter case \(I_i\ge\varepsilon/q\) for \(i=1,\dots,5\), while always
\[
\sum_i w_i I_i\le 1-\varepsilon/p.
\]
It does not solve the original problem of determining all \(p\) for which the inequality holds for arbitrary nonnegative weights.

statement: Berry-Esseen effective-dimension criterion and limitations of crude finite-head certificates.
Berry-Esseen effective-dimension criterion and limitations of crude finite-head certificates.
proof_excerpt: By centering and rescaling the Bernoulli sum into a standardized variable \(Z=(S-p)/\sqrt{pq\sum w_i^2}\), the proof applies the sharp weighted Berry–Esseen bound with absolute constant 0.56 to show that \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge\Phi(0)-\delta=\tfrac12-\delta\), and then optimizes over \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) to deduce the effective-dimension condition \(0.56\,\kappa(p)\,\rho_0\le\tfrac12-p\), i.e.\ \(\rho_0\le5\sqrt2/28\).  The same Berry–Esseen estimate, combined with a head/buffer/tail decomposition and a “\(1/32\)-regular” bound on the third-moment ratio of the tail, guarantees that the Gaussian tail at threshold \(1/3\) exceeds the error term, yielding the small-head-plus-buffer criterion.  Finally, explicit weight constructions show that if either the range/mean buffer or the no-buffer Gaussian certificate is used in isolation (i.e.\ if its corresponding \(\rho\)-bound is violated), then \(\Pr[S\ge p]\) can fall strictly below \(p\).
problem_solved: Assuming the one-dimensional Berry–Esseen inequality with absolute constant \(0.56\), the proof establishes the stated partial proposition: for finite nonnegative weights summing to \(1\) and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables, if \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) and \(\rho_0=\sum_i w_i^3/(\sum_i w_i^2)^{3/2}\le 5\sqrt2/28\), then \(\Pr[S\ge p]\ge p\). It also establishes the strict head/buffer/remainder sufficient condition when \(B_R>0\), \(\max_{i\in I_R}w_i\le (1/32)\sqrt{B_R}\), \(\sigma=\sqrt{p(1-p)B_R}\), and \(\alpha+\gamma\le\sigma\). Finally, it correctly verifies the two advertised certificate failures: the fourteen-equal-weight range/mean-only buffer computation gives \(49159/255879<1/3\), and the \(m=1033\) no-buffer Berry–Esseen Gaussian certificate gives \(G-0.56\,\kappa(1/3)\rho_R<1/3\). It does not characterize all \(p\) for the original problem.

statement: Strict reduced support-9 theorem in the upper interval: for p in [1/4,1/3), positive weights 0p]>p.
proof_excerpt: By encoding the event {∑wᵢvᵢ≤p} as q⁹∑_{j=0}⁹ d_j ρʲ (with ρ=p/(1–p)), the proof builds a threshold graph W on the nine weights so that independent and intersecting‐family extremal lemmas (for triples, 4‐sets and triangles) together with a cascade of “weight‐deficiency’’ inequalities tightly bound d₃–d₆ by explicit linear caps.  This reduces the probabilistic inequality ∑d_j ρʲ<(1+ρ)⁸ to a single‐variable polynomial check on ρ∈[1/3,1/2), which is then verified exhaustively by a finite, computer‐assisted Sturm‐root analysis of all extreme cases.
problem_solved:
Assuming the finite verification lemma is accepted, the proof establishes the reduced theorem: for every \(p\in[1/4,1/3)\), every positive \(w_1,\dots,w_9\) with \(w_i<p\) and \(\sum_i w_i=1\), and independent Bernoulli\((p)\) variables \(v_i\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i>p\right]>p.
\]
Consequently, under the same restricted hypotheses,
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^9 w_i v_i\ge p\right]>p,
\]
hence certainly \(\ge p\). The proof does not establish the original problem for arbitrary \(m\), merely nonnegative weights, weights allowed to be \(\ge p\), or \(p\)-ranges outside \([1/4,1/3)\).

statement: The inequality holds for p=0, p=1/3, p=1/2, and p=1; it fails for every p in (1/3,1/2) and every p in (1/2,1).
proof_sketch: The endpoint cases use complement/coloring arguments at p=1/3 and p=1/2; the counterexamples use equal weights on 3 coordinates for (1/3,1/2) and equal weights on 2 coordinates for (1/2,1).

statement: If B(r) holds and r=kp for an integer k with r<=1, then B(p) holds; hence proving B(p) on [1/6,1/3] proves B(p) on [0,1/3].
proof_sketch: Use a categorical coupling with k nonzero colors of probability p each and apply B(r) to the union of those colors.

statement: For p in [1/6,1/3), any strict reduced counterexample must have at least 10 positive weights.
proof_sketch: The verified strict support-9 theorem rules out exactly nine positive weights; fewer positive weights can be split into nine positive weights while preserving strict bad probability for sufficiently small splits.

statement: For p=1/k, the inequality holds for all weights.
proof_sketch: Color coordinates uniformly with k colors; on every color-orbit the k color sums add to 1, so at least one is at least 1/k, and symmetry gives probability at least 1/k.

statement: In a minimum-support strict reduced counterexample with bad probability q+epsilon, every pair has level-2 p-biased Fourier coefficient at least epsilon.
proof_sketch: Merging any pair gives a non-counterexample, either by minimality or because the merged weight is at least p; the exact merge formula identifies the merge gap with the level-2 coefficient.

statement: Any such minimum-support counterexample satisfies epsilon^2[(q/p) sum_i ((1-w_i)/w_i)^2 + binom(n,2)] <= (p-epsilon)(q+epsilon), hence epsilon < 1/(3(n-1)sqrt(n)) for p<=1/3.
proof_sketch: Pair coefficients contribute level-2 Fourier mass; pivotal influence lower bounds contribute level-1 Fourier mass; Parseval bounds the total by Var(1_G).

statement: The one-coordinate tail-ratio inequality q P[T>=p] >= p P[T<p-a] is exactly sufficient to prove B(p) for p<=1/3.
proof_sketch: Condition on a coordinate of weight a: P[av+T>=p]-p = q P[T>=p]-p P[T<p-a].

approach: Tail-ratio lemma in arbitrary support
reason: The lemma is proved for reciprocal p, large tail atom, and tail support at most 3, but no arbitrary-support proof was obtained.

approach: Pair-merge induction
reason: Merging is not universally monotone; a minimum counterexample would force all pair merges to be bad rather than yielding an immediate reducible pair.

approach: Fourier/Parseval contradiction
reason: All-pair positivity gives strong small-epsilon bounds, but the resulting inequalities still allow a tiny positive epsilon.

approach: Finite-support elimination
reason: Verified layer-counting eliminates support up to 9 in the base interval, but the remaining support n>=10 case is not closed.

statement:
After adding the integer condition in the bootstrap step, the proof establishes the advertised partial theorem: \(B(0)\) holds; \(B(1/k)\) holds for every positive integer \(k\); \(B(p)\) fails for \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\); and if \(k\in\mathbb N\), \(kp\le1\), and \(B(kp)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.

It also proves the one-coordinate reduction and the stated structural constraints for every strict reduced minimum-support counterexample: the pair-merge bound \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\), the influence identity \(\widehat F(\{i\})=\sqrt{pq}\,I_i\), the inequalities \(\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i\), \(w_i\ge\varepsilon/p\), \(\varepsilon\le p/n\), the Parseval lower bound, the consequence \(\varepsilon<1/[3(n-1)\sqrt n]\) when \(p\le1/3\), and the antichain bound \(\varepsilon\le pM_{n-1}(p)/(n-1)\), including the \(n=10\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) estimate \(\varepsilon\le1792/177147<0.0102\).

It does not solve the original problem completely: it does not prove \(B(p)\) for all non-reciprocal \(p\in(0,1/3)\), nor does it give a valid support-\(9\) elimination.
proof_excerpt: We first establish the endpoint and reciprocal cases \(B(1/k)\) by a random-coloring argument and then bootstrap from \(B(kp)\) to \(B(p)\) via a multicolor union bound on coordinate classes.  The main difficulty—ruling out a strict reduced counterexample for \(p\le1/3\)—is overcome by a Fourier-analytic “pair-merge” lemma showing all level-2 coefficients \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\), together with an influence identity for level-1 coefficients and Parseval; these yield a lower bound on \(\varepsilon\) that, when combined with Jensen’s convexity bound and an antichain (LYM) estimate on pivotal sets, forces \(\varepsilon\) below any positive threshold (notably for support ≤9), thus eliminating counterexamples.
problem_solved:
After adding the integer condition in the bootstrap step, the proof establishes the advertised partial theorem: \(B(0)\) holds; \(B(1/k)\) holds for every positive integer \(k\); \(B(p)\) fails for \(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\); and if \(k\in\mathbb N\), \(kp\le1\), and \(B(kp)\) holds, then \(B(p)\) holds.

It also proves the one-coordinate reduction and the stated structural constraints for every strict reduced minimum-support counterexample: the pair-merge bound \(\widehat F(\{i,j\})\ge\varepsilon\), the influence identity \(\widehat F(\{i\})=\sqrt{pq}\,I_i\), the inequalities \(\varepsilon(1-w_i)\le p w_i I_i\), \(w_i\ge\varepsilon/p\), \(\varepsilon\le p/n\), the Parseval lower bound, the consequence \(\varepsilon<1/[3(n-1)\sqrt n]\) when \(p\le1/3\), and the antichain bound \(\varepsilon\le pM_{n-1}(p)/(n-1)\), including the \(n=10\), \(p\in[1/4,1/3]\) estimate \(\varepsilon\le1792/177147<0.0102\).

It does not solve the original problem completely: it does not prove \(B(p)\) for all non-reciprocal \(p\in(0,1/3)\), nor does it give a valid support-\(9\) elimination.
